title: Wolverhampton

infobox.value: List   * [Aldersley](/wiki/Aldersley "Aldersley") * [All Saints](/wiki/All_Saints,_Wolverhampton "All Saints, Wolverhampton") * [Blakenhall](/wiki/Blakenhall "Blakenhall") * [Bradmore](/wiki/Bradmore,_West_Midlands "Bradmore, West Midlands") * [Chapel Ash](/wiki/Chapel_Ash "Chapel Ash") * [Claregate](/wiki/Claregate "Claregate") * [Compton](/wiki/Compton,_Wolverhampton "Compton, Wolverhampton") * [Deansfield](/w/index.php?title=Deansfield,_West_Midlands&action=edit&redlink=1 "Deansfield, West Midlands (page does not exist)") * [Dunstall Hill](/wiki/Dunstall_Hill "Dunstall Hill") * [East Park](/wiki/East_Park,_Wolverhampton "East Park, Wolverhampton") * [Ettingshall](/wiki/Ettingshall "Ettingshall") * [Fallings Park](/wiki/Fallings_Park "Fallings Park") * [Gorsebrook](/w/index.php?title=Gorsebrook,_West_Midlands&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gorsebrook, West Midlands (page does not exist)") * [Graiseley](/wiki/Graiseley "Graiseley") * [Heath Town](/wiki/Heath_Town "Heath Town") * [Horseley Fields](/wiki/Horseley_Fields "Horseley Fields") * [Merridale](/wiki/Merridale "Merridale") * [Monmore Green](/wiki/Monmore_Green "Monmore Green") * [Newbridge](/wiki/Newbridge,_Wolverhampton "Newbridge, Wolverhampton") * [Old Fallings](/wiki/Old_Fallings "Old Fallings") * [Park Village](/wiki/Park_Village "Park Village") * [Scotlands](/wiki/Scotlands "Scotlands") * [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Wolverhampton "Springfield, Wolverhampton") * [Stow Heath](/wiki/Stow_Heath "Stow Heath") * [Tettenhall](/wiki/Tettenhall "Tettenhall") (village) * [Tettenhall Wood](/wiki/Tettenhall_Wood "Tettenhall Wood") * [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield") (town) * [Whitmore Reans](/wiki/Whitmore_Reans "Whitmore Reans")

List   * 60.6% [White](/wiki/White_people_in_the_United_Kingdom "White people in the United Kingdom") * 21.2% [Asian](/wiki/British_Asians "British Asians") * 9.3% [Black](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people") * 5.3% [Mixed](/wiki/Mixed_(United_Kingdom_ethnicity_category) "Mixed (United Kingdom ethnicity category)") * 3.6% [other](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_United_Kingdom "Ethnic groups in the United Kingdom")

List   * 43.8% [Christianity](/wiki/Religion_in_England#Christianity "Religion in England") * 27.8% [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_United_Kingdom "Irreligion in the United Kingdom") * 12% [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism_in_England "Sikhism in England") * 5.5% [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_England "Islam in England") * 5.5% not stated * 3.7% [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_England "Hinduism in England") * 1.2% [other](/wiki/Religion_in_England "Religion in England") * 0.3% [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_England "Buddhism in England") * 0.1% [Judaism](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_England "History of the Jews in England")

([/ˌwʊlvərˈhæmptən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-Wolverhampton.ogg "File:En-uk-Wolverhampton.ogg") [*WUUL-vər-HAMP-tən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [city](/wiki/City "City") and [metropolitan borough](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough") in the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") of England. Located around 12 miles (20 km) northwest of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), it forms the northwestern part of the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation"), with the towns of [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall") to the east and [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley") to the south. The population in 2021 was 263,700, making it the third largest city in the West Midlands after Birmingham and [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry").

**Wolverhampton** ([/ˌwʊlvərˈhæmptən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-uk-Wolverhampton.ogg "File:En-uk-Wolverhampton.ogg") [*WUUL-vər-HAMP-tən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [city](/wiki/City "City") and [metropolitan borough](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough") in the [West Midlands](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)") of England. Located around 12 miles (20 km) northwest of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), it forms the northwestern part of the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation"), with the towns of [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall") to the east and [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley") to the south. The population in 2021 was 263,700, making it the third largest city in the West Midlands after Birmingham and [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry").

[Historically](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") in [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"), Wolverhampton grew as a [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") specialising in the [wool](/wiki/Wool "Wool") trade. During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), it became a major centre for coal mining, steel production, lock making, and automotive manufacturing; the economy of the city is still based on engineering, including a large [aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace") industry, as well as the [service sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy"). The city is also home to the [University of Wolverhampton](/wiki/University_of_Wolverhampton "University of Wolverhampton"). A town for most of its history, it gained [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") in 2001. The [M6 motorway](/wiki/M6_motorway "M6 motorway") runs just outside Wolverhampton's east and northeast borders, while the [M54 motorway](/wiki/M54_motorway "M54 motorway") starts to the north and links the city with [Telford](/wiki/Telford "Telford"). For railway travel, the city is served by [Wolverhampton station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_station "Wolverhampton station"), while the western terminus of the [West Midlands Metro](/wiki/West_Midlands_Metro "West Midlands Metro") network is also located in the city.

Wolverhampton has produced many notable musicians and artists and is known in the sports world as the home of [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") football team [Wolverhampton Wanderers FC](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C."), which plays at [Molineux Stadium](/wiki/Molineux_Stadium "Molineux Stadium"). As a result of experiencing a heavy influx of Indian immigrants from the 1950s onwards, the city is home to the largest percentage of [Sikhs in England](/wiki/Sikhism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Sikhism in the United Kingdom"), who have been influential in shaping its identity; it also hosts a higher percentage of [Hindus](/wiki/Hinduism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Hinduism in the United Kingdom") than the national average.

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The city is named after [Wulfrun](/wiki/Wulfrun "Wulfrun"), who founded the town in 985, from the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *Wulfrūnehēantūn* ('Wulfrūn's high or principal enclosure or farm').[[4]](#cite_note-farley-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Horovitz-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Before the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") of 1066, the area's name appears only as variants of *Heantune* or *Hamtun*; the prefix *Wulfrun* or similar appearing in 1070 and thereafter.[[5]](#cite_note-Horovitz-5) Alternatively, the city may have earned its original name from *Wulfereēantūn* ('[Wulfhere](/wiki/Wulfhere_of_Mercia "Wulfhere of Mercia")'s high or principal enclosure or farm') after the [Mercian](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") King,[[7]](#cite_note-7) who according to tradition established an abbey in 659, though no evidence of an abbey has been found.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The variation *Wolveren Hampton* is seen in medieval records, for example in 1381.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* includes the [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") *Wulfrunian*, defined as "An inhabitant of Wolverhampton", its earliest example of use being from 1959.[[2]](#cite_note-oed-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

A local tradition states that King [Wulfhere of Mercia](/wiki/Wulfhere_of_Mercia "Wulfhere of Mercia") founded an [abbey](/wiki/Abbey "Abbey") of St Mary at Wolverhampton in 659.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Wolverhampton is recorded as being the site of a [decisive battle](/wiki/Battle_of_Tettenhall "Battle of Tettenhall") between the unified Mercian Angles and West Saxons against the raiding Danes in 910, although sources are unclear as to whether the battle itself took place in [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield") or [Tettenhall](/wiki/Tettenhall "Tettenhall").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Both places have since been incorporated into Wolverhampton. The Mercians and West Saxons claimed a decisive victory, and the field of Woden is recognised by numerous place names in Wednesfield.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Lady_wulfruna.jpg/250px-Lady_wulfruna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_wulfruna.jpg)

Statue of [Lady Wulfrun](/wiki/Lady_Wulfruna "Lady Wulfruna") on western side of [St. Peter's Collegiate Church](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Collegiate_Church,_Wolverhampton "St. Peter's Collegiate Church, Wolverhampton")

In 985, King [Ethelred the Unready](/wiki/Ethelred_the_Unready "Ethelred the Unready") granted lands at a place referred to as *Heantun* to Lady Wulfrun by royal charter,[[14]](#cite_note-Wulfruna-14) and hence founding the settlement.

In 994, a [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") was consecrated in Wolverhampton for which Wulfrun granted land at [Upper Arley](/wiki/Upper_Arley "Upper Arley") in Worcestershire, [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston"), [Willenhall](/wiki/Willenhall "Willenhall"), Wednesfield, [Pelsall](/wiki/Pelsall "Pelsall"), [Ogley Hay](/wiki/Ogley_Hay "Ogley Hay") near [Brownhills](/wiki/Brownhills "Brownhills"), Hilton near [Wall](/wiki/Wall,_Staffordshire "Wall, Staffordshire"), [Hatherton](/wiki/Hatherton,_Staffordshire "Hatherton, Staffordshire"), [Kinvaston](/w/index.php?title=Kinvaston&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kinvaston (page does not exist)"), Hilton near Wolverhampton, and [Featherstone](/wiki/Featherstone,_Staffordshire "Featherstone, Staffordshire").[[14]](#cite_note-Wulfruna-14) This became the site for the current [St. Peter's Church](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_Collegiate_Church "St Peter's Collegiate Church").[[15]](#cite_note-WHHS-15) A statue of Lady Wulfrun, sculpted by [Sir Charles Wheeler](/wiki/Charles_Wheeler_(sculptor) "Charles Wheeler (sculptor)"), can be seen on the stairs outside the church.[[14]](#cite_note-Wulfruna-14)

Wolverhampton is recorded in the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") in 1086 as being in the [Hundred](/wiki/Hundred_(county_division) "Hundred (county division)") of [Seisdon](/wiki/Seisdon "Seisdon") and the county of [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire"). The lords of the manor are listed as the canons of St Mary (the church's dedication was changed to St Peter after this date), with the tenant-in-chief being [Samson](/wiki/Samson_(bishop_of_Worcester) "Samson (bishop of Worcester)"), [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror")'s personal chaplain.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Wolverhampton at this date is a large settlement of fifty households.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 1179, there is mention of a market held in the town, and in 1204 it had come to the attention of [King John](/wiki/John_of_England "John of England") that the town did not possess a Royal Charter for holding a market. This charter for a weekly market held on a Wednesday was eventually granted on 4 February 1258 by [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England").[[15]](#cite_note-WHHS-15)

It is held that in the 14th and 15th centuries that Wolverhampton was one of the "staple towns" of the woollen trade,[[15]](#cite_note-WHHS-15) which today can be seen by the inclusion of a woolpack on the city's coat of arms,[[18]](#cite_note-Coat_of_arms-18) and by the many small streets, especially in the city centre, called "Fold" (examples being Blossom's Fold, Farmers Fold, Townwell Fold and Victoria Fold), as well as Woolpack Street and Woolpack Alley.[[15]](#cite_note-WHHS-15)

In 1512, Sir [Stephen Jenyns](/wiki/Stephen_Jenyns "Stephen Jenyns"), a former [Lord Mayor of London](/wiki/Lord_Mayor_of_London "Lord Mayor of London") and a twice Master of the [Worshipful Company of Merchant Taylors](/wiki/Worshipful_Company_of_Merchant_Taylors "Worshipful Company of Merchant Taylors"), who was born in the city, founded [Wolverhampton Grammar School](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Grammar_School "Wolverhampton Grammar School"), one of the oldest active schools in Britain.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

From the 16th century onwards, Wolverhampton became home to a number of metal industries including lock and [key](/wiki/Key_(lock) "Key (lock)") making and iron and [brass](/wiki/Brass "Brass") working.

Wolverhampton suffered two Great Fires: the first in April 1590, and the second in September 1696. Both fires started in today's Salop Street. The first fire lasted for five days and left nearly 700 people homeless, whilst the second destroyed 60 homes in the first five hours. This second fire led to the purchase of the first [fire engine](/wiki/Fire_engine "Fire engine") within the city in September 1703.[[15]](#cite_note-WHHS-15)

On 27 January 1606,[[20]](#cite_note-20) two farmers, Thomas Smart and John Holyhead of [Rowley Regis](/wiki/Rowley_Regis "Rowley Regis"), were executed on High Green, now Queen Square, for sheltering two of the [Gunpowder Plotters](/wiki/Gunpowder_Plot "Gunpowder Plot"), [Robert Wintour](/wiki/Robert_Wintour "Robert Wintour") and [Stephen Littleton](/wiki/Gunpowder_Plot#Last_stand "Gunpowder Plot"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) who had fled to the Midlands. The pair played no part in the original plot nevertheless suffered a traitor's death of being [hanged, drawn and quartered](/wiki/Hanged,_drawn_and_quartered "Hanged, drawn and quartered") on butcher's blocks set up in the square a few days before the execution of [Guy Fawkes](/wiki/Guy_Fawkes "Guy Fawkes") and several other plotters in London.[[15]](#cite_note-WHHS-15)

There is also evidence that Wolverhampton may have been the location of the first working [Newcomen Steam Engine](/wiki/Newcomen_atmospheric_engine "Newcomen atmospheric engine") in 1712.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Wightwick_Manor_02.jpg/330px-Wightwick_Manor_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wightwick_Manor_02.jpg)

[Wightwick Manor](/wiki/Wightwick_Manor "Wightwick Manor")

A few years before she began her reign, [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") visited Wolverhampton in the 1830s and described it as "a large and dirty town" but one which received her "with great friendliness and pleasure". In [Victorian times](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era"), Wolverhampton grew to be a wealthy town mainly due to the huge amount of industry that occurred as a result of the abundance of coal and iron deposits in the area. The remains of this wealth can be seen in local houses such as [Wightwick Manor](/wiki/Wightwick_Manor "Wightwick Manor") and The Mount (both built for the prominent varnish and paint manufacturers, the [Mander family](/wiki/Mander_family "Mander family")) as well as Tettenhall Towers. All three are located in the western fringe of Wolverhampton, in the areas known as Wightwick and Tettenhall. Many other houses of similar stature were demolished in the 1960s and 1970s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Prince_Albert_Wolverhampton.jpg/330px-Prince_Albert_Wolverhampton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_Albert_Wolverhampton.jpg)

Statue of Prince Albert in Queen Square

Wolverhampton gained its first parliamentary representation as part of the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"), when it was one of 22 large towns that were allocated two members of parliament. A local mob attacking electors who voted or intended to vote for the [Tory](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") candidate led to the [1835 Wolverhampton riot](/wiki/1835_Wolverhampton_riot "1835 Wolverhampton riot"), with [dragoons](/wiki/Dragoon "Dragoon") being called in to end the intimidation. Wolverhampton was incorporated as a [municipal borough](/wiki/Municipal_borough "Municipal borough") on 15 March 1848 under the [Municipal Corporations Act 1835](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_Act_1835 "Municipal Corporations Act 1835") before becoming a [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") in 1889.[[23]](#cite_note-woodfield-23) It was represented politically in Victorian times by [Charles Pelham Villiers](/wiki/Charles_Pelham_Villiers "Charles Pelham Villiers"), a [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") [MP](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament") and noted [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") supporter who was also the longest-serving MP in parliamentary history. [Lord Wolverhampton, Henry Hartley Fowler](/wiki/Henry_Fowler,_1st_Viscount_Wolverhampton "Henry Fowler, 1st Viscount Wolverhampton") was MP for Wolverhampton at the turn of the century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The railways reached Wolverhampton in 1837, with the first station located at [Wednesfield Heath](/wiki/Wednesfield_Heath_railway_station "Wednesfield Heath railway station"), now Heath Town, on the [Grand Junction Railway](/wiki/Grand_Junction_Railway "Grand Junction Railway").[[24]](#cite_note-Virgin_Trains-24) This station was demolished in 1965, but the area exists as a nature reserve just off Powell Street.[[25]](#cite_note-Heath_Town_Station-25) [Wolverhampton railway works](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_works "Wolverhampton railway works") was established in 1849 for the [Shrewsbury and Birmingham Railway](/wiki/Shrewsbury_and_Birmingham_Railway "Shrewsbury and Birmingham Railway") and became the Northern Division workshop of the [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") in 1854.[[26]](#cite_note-manufacturing-26)

In the 19th century, the city saw much immigration from [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), the latter following the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)"). In 1866, a statue was erected in memory of [Prince Albert the Prince Consort](/wiki/Albert,_Prince_Consort "Albert, Prince Consort"), the unveiling of which brought Queen Victoria back to Wolverhampton.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The unveiling of the statue was the first public appearance Queen Victoria made after the funeral of her husband. A 40-foot-tall (12 m) archway made of coal was constructed for the visit. The Queen was so pleased with the statue that she knighted Wolverhampton's mayor at the time, an industrialist named [John Morris](/w/index.php?title=John_Morris_(industrialist)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John Morris (industrialist) (page does not exist)"). Market Square, originally named High Green, was renamed Queen Square in honour of the visit. The statue replaced a Russian cannon captured during the [Siege of Sevastopol](/wiki/Siege_of_Sevastopol_(1854%E2%80%9355) "Siege of Sevastopol (1854–55)") in 1855,[[23]](#cite_note-woodfield-23) and remains standing in Queen Square. The statue is known locally as "The Man on the Horse".

The [Stafford Street drill hall](/wiki/Stafford_Street_drill_hall,_Wolverhampton "Stafford Street drill hall, Wolverhampton") was completed in 1890.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Wolverhampton had a prolific bicycle industry from 1868 to 1975, during which time a total of more than 200 bicycle manufacturing companies existed, none remaining today. These manufacturers included Viking, Marston, Sunbeam, Star, Wulfruna and Rudge.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The last volume manufacturers of bicycles left Wolverhampton during the 1960s and 1970s – the largest and best-known of which was Viking Cycles Ltd,[[30]](#cite_note-30) whose team dominated the UK racing scene in the 1950s (Viking's production of hand-built lightweight racing and juvenile bicycles exceeded 20,000 units in 1965). Closures of other smaller cycle makers followed during the 1980s including such well-known hand-builders as [Percy Stallard](/wiki/Percy_Stallard "Percy Stallard") (the former professional cyclist) and Jack Hateley.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[Wolverhampton High Level station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_station "Wolverhampton railway station") (the current main railway station) opened in 1852, but the original station was demolished in 1965 and then rebuilt.[[32]](#cite_note-highlevelstation-32) [Wolverhampton Low Level station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Low_Level_railway_station "Wolverhampton Low Level railway station") opened on the Great Western Railway in 1855. The site of the Low Level station, which closed to passengers in 1972 and completely in 1981, has since been redeveloped with much of the original station incorporated into a hotel. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1918 at "The Mount" in [Tettenhall Wood](/wiki/Tettenhall_Wood "Tettenhall Wood"), the British Prime Minister [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") announced a general election.[[33]](#cite_note-33) He also made his "Homes fit for heroes" speech at Wolverhampton Grand Theatre in the same year.[[34]](#cite_note-34) It was on the idea of "Homes fit for heroes" that Lloyd George was to fight the [1918 "Coupon" General Election](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election "1918 United Kingdom general election").

Mass [council housing](/wiki/Council_housing "Council housing") development in Wolverhampton, to rehouse families from slum housing, began after the end of the [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). New estates at Parkfields (near the border with [Coseley](/wiki/Coseley "Coseley")) and Birches Barn (near Bantock Park in the west of Wolverhampton) gave the city some 550 new council houses by 1923, although this was a fraction of the number required. The first large council housing development in Wolverhampton was the [Low Hill](/wiki/Low_Hill "Low Hill") estate to the north-east of the city, which consisted of more than 2,000 new council houses by 1927 and was one of the largest housing estates in Britain at the time.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Mass council housing development in Wolverhampton continued into the 1930s, mostly in the north of the city in the [Oxley](/wiki/Oxley,_Wolverhampton "Oxley, Wolverhampton") and [Wobaston](/w/index.php?title=Wobaston&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wobaston (page does not exist)") areas and on the new [Scotlands Estate](/wiki/Scotlands_Estate "Scotlands Estate") in the north-east. However, council house building halted in 1940 following the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in September the previous year.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[Wolverhampton St George's](/wiki/Wolverhampton_St_George%27s_tram_stop "Wolverhampton St George's tram stop") (in the city centre) was the northern terminus for the [West Midlands Metro](/wiki/West_Midlands_Metro "West Midlands Metro") light rail system, now terminating at [Wolverhampton station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_station "Wolverhampton station"). An extension to the railway station opened in 2023, the opening being delayed until the new railway station was completed. Wolverhampton was one of the few towns to operate surface contact trams and the only town to use the Lorain Surface Contact System.[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus") appeared in 1923, and in 1930 for a brief period the [Wolverhampton trolleybus system](/wiki/Trolleybuses_in_Wolverhampton "Trolleybuses in Wolverhampton") was the world's largest trolleybus system.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The last Wolverhampton trolleybus ran in 1967, just as the railway line through the High Level station was converted to electric operation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Wolverhampton_Princes_Square.JPG/250px-Wolverhampton_Princes_Square.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wolverhampton_Princes_Square.JPG)

The location of the UK's first set of traffic lights at Princes Square; the poles are painted with black and white bands, as they were originally

England's first automatic [traffic lights](/wiki/Traffic_light "Traffic light") could be seen in Princes Square in 1927.[[39]](#cite_note-Wolverhampton_City_Council-39) The modern traffic lights at this location have the traditional striped poles to commemorate this fact. Princes Square was also the location of the United Kingdom's first pedestrian safety barriers, which were erected in 1934.[[40]](#cite_note-Steelway_Limited-40) On 2 November 1927, the [A4123](/wiki/A4123_road "A4123 road") New Road was opened by the then-Prince of Wales (later [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII"))[[41]](#cite_note-41) linking the city with [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"). The New Road was designed as an unemployment relief project[[42]](#cite_note-42) and was the United Kingdom's first purpose-built intercity highway of the twentieth century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Sir Geoffrey Le Mesurier Mander](/wiki/Geoffrey_Mander "Geoffrey Mander"), a member of the [Mander family](/wiki/Mander_family "Mander family"), was Liberal MP for [Wolverhampton East](/wiki/Wolverhampton_East_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wolverhampton East (UK Parliament constituency)") from 1929 to 1945, distinguished for his stance against [appeasement](/wiki/Appeasement "Appeasement") and as a supporter of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"). He was known as "the last of the Midland radicals". More recent members have included the [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") mavericks [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell") and [Nicholas Budgen](/wiki/Nicholas_Budgen "Nicholas Budgen"). Powell was a member of [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath")'s Tory shadow cabinet from 1964, until he was dismissed in April 1968 following his controversial [Rivers of Blood speech](/wiki/Rivers_of_Blood_speech "Rivers of Blood speech") in which he warned of massive civil unrest if mass immigration of black and Asian [commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") inhabitants continued. At the same period, Sikh bus drivers and conductors were demonstrating in Wolverhampton against the Transportation Committee's regulations requiring uniform caps and thus prohibiting turbans.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In 2005, former [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston") councillor and MP for [Wolverhampton South East](/wiki/Wolverhampton_South_East_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wolverhampton South East (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Dennis Turner](/wiki/Dennis_Turner,_Baron_Bilston "Dennis Turner, Baron Bilston") entered the House of Lords as Lord Bilston.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Following the end of World War II in 1945, the council erected 400 prefabricated bungalows across Wolverhampton, and built its first permanent postwar houses at the Underhill Estate near [Bushbury](/wiki/Bushbury "Bushbury") in the late 1940s.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The 1950s saw many new houses and flats built across Wolverhampton as the rehousing programme from the slums continued, as well as the local council agreeing deals with neighbouring authorities [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield") Urban District and [Seisdon Rural District](/wiki/Seisdon_Rural_District "Seisdon Rural District") which saw families relocated to new estates in those areas.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The 1960s saw the rehousing programme continue, with multi-storey blocks being built on a large scale across Wolverhampton at locations including [Blakenhall](/wiki/Blakenhall "Blakenhall"), [Whitmore Reans](/wiki/Whitmore_Reans "Whitmore Reans") and [Chetton Green](/w/index.php?title=Chetton_Green&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chetton Green (page does not exist)"). The later part of the decade saw the [Heath Town](/wiki/Heath_Town "Heath Town") district almost completely redeveloped with multi-story flats and maisonette blocks.[[46]](#cite_note-46) By 1975, by which time Wolverhampton had also taken in the majority of the Borough of [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston"), the Urban Districts of [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield") and [Tettenhall](/wiki/Tettenhall "Tettenhall") and parts of [Willenhall](/wiki/Willenhall "Willenhall"), [Sedgley](/wiki/Sedgley "Sedgley") and [Coseley](/wiki/Coseley "Coseley"), almost a third of Wolverhampton's population lived in council housing, but since that date social housing has been built on a minimal scale in the area, and some of the 1919–1975 developments have since been demolished.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

As well as the many new council estates which sprang up around Wolverhampton during the 20th century, several older parts of the town were redeveloped for new council housing during the 1960s and early 1970s. The most notable example is the Heath Town area, where almost all of the 19th-century buildings were demolished during the 1960s and replaced by four tower blocks and several blocks of maisonettes. However, the state housing at Heath Town quickly became unpopular and by the 1980s the area was plagued with crime and unemployment. The first regeneration projects on the estate began during the 1990s, and in 2017 some of the maisonette blocks were demolished. A similar redevelopment took place around the same time in Blakenhall, where new shops and five tower blocks were built in a 1960s redevelopment area. However, all of these buildings were demolished between 2002 and 2011 and have since been replaced with new private and social housing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Large numbers of black and Asian immigrants settled in Wolverhampton from the 1950s and 1960s, mostly in the [Blakenhall](/wiki/Blakenhall "Blakenhall"), [All Saints](/wiki/All_Saints,_Wolverhampton "All Saints, Wolverhampton"), [Whitmore Reans](/wiki/Whitmore_Reans "Whitmore Reans") and [Heath Town](/wiki/Heath_Town "Heath Town") areas. Wolverhampton is home to a large [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") community, who settled there during the period (1935–1975) from the Indian state of [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"). Today, the Sikh community in Wolverhampton is roughly 12% of the city's population.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1974, as a result of local government reorganisation, Wolverhampton became a [metropolitan borough](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough"), transferring from the ceremonial Staffordshire into the newly formed West Midlands county; though it is important to note that as a [County Borough](/wiki/County_Borough "County Borough"), it was never administered by Staffordshire County Council. Wolverhampton was granted [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"), announced on 18 December 2000[[48]](#cite_note-48) and formally conferred on 31 January 2001[[49]](#cite_note-49) – an honour that had been unsuccessfully applied for in 1953, 1966, 1977,[[50]](#cite_note-citystatus-50) 1985[[51]](#cite_note-51) and 1992[[50]](#cite_note-citystatus-50) – making it one of three "Millennium Cities".[[52]](#cite_note-52) Wolverhampton also made an unsuccessful application for a Lord Mayor in 2002.[[50]](#cite_note-citystatus-50)

Many of the city centre's buildings date from the early 20th century and before, the oldest buildings being [St Peter's Church](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_Collegiate_Church "St Peter's Collegiate Church") (which was built in the 13th century but has been largely extended and refurbished since the 15th century, situated on Lichfield Street)[[53]](#cite_note-53) and a framed timber 17th-century building on Victoria Street which is now one of just two remaining in the area which was heavily populated by them until the turn of the 20th century. This building was originally a residential property, but later became the Hand Inn public house. Later becoming Lindy Lou's children's shop and still called Lindy Lou's by locals. It carries a "false" date of 1300AD fitted during a past refurbishment.
It was completely restored in 1981 after a two-year refurbishment project and has been used by various businesses since then including as a second-hand book shop.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

On 23 November 1981, [an F1/T2 tornado](/wiki/1981_United_Kingdom_tornado_outbreak "1981 United Kingdom tornado outbreak") touched down in [Fordhouses](/wiki/Fordhouses "Fordhouses") to the north of Wolverhampton, and later moved over Wolverhampton city centre and surrounding suburbs, causing some damage.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

The [Wolverhampton Ring Road](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Ring_Road "Wolverhampton Ring Road") circumnavigates the city centre linking the majority of the city's radial routes. It was constructed in sections between 1960 and 1986, and carries the number A4150, although this is only marked on one road sign.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The centre of Wolverhampton has been altered radically since the mid-1960s, with the Mander Centre (plans for which were unveiled on 15 April 1965)[[56]](#cite_note-56) being opened in two phases, the first in 1968 and the second in 1971. Several refurbishments have taken place since. The Wulfrun Centre, an open shopping area, was opened alongside the Mander Centre's first phase in 1968, but has been undercover since a roof was added in the late 1990s.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Central Wolverhampton police station was built just south of the city centre on Birmingham Road during the 1960s, but operations there were cut back in the early 1990s when a new larger police station was built on Bilston Street on land which became vacant a decade earlier on the demolition of a factory along with a number of shops and the Clifton Cinema. This was officially opened by [Diana, Princess of Wales](/wiki/Diana,_Princess_of_Wales "Diana, Princess of Wales"), on 31 July 1992.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The city centre had several cinemas during the 20th century, including the [Odeon Cinema](/wiki/Odeon_Cinema,_Wolverhampton "Odeon Cinema, Wolverhampton"). The last of these was the ABC Cinema (formerly the Savoy), which closed in 1991 after 54 years. It was then converted into a nightclub, with part of the site being converted into the offices of a [recruitment agency](/wiki/Recruitment_agency "Recruitment agency") in 2005.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The building was demolished in 2019 to make way for an extension to the [City of Wolverhampton College](/wiki/City_of_Wolverhampton_College "City of Wolverhampton College")'s Metro One campus.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

A modern landmark in the city centre is the [Wolverhampton Combined Court Centre](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Combined_Court_Centre "Wolverhampton Combined Court Centre") on Pipers Row, which opened in 1990 as the town's first purpose-built crown court.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 21st century")]

A few department store chains including [Marks & Spencer](/wiki/Marks_%26_Spencer "Marks & Spencer") and [Next](/wiki/Next_plc "Next plc") have stores in the centre of Wolverhampton. [Beatties](/wiki/Beatties "Beatties"), a [House of Fraser](/wiki/House_of_Fraser "House of Fraser") store, was announced to close in 2019.[[62]](#cite_note-eas20180607-62) [Debenhams](/wiki/Debenhams "Debenhams") opened a 3-floor department store in the [Mander Centre](/wiki/Mander_Centre "Mander Centre") in 2017, but has now closed.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In 2021, a [blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque") was erected in memory of British immigrant rights activist [Paulette Wilson](/wiki/Paulette_Wilson "Paulette Wilson"), a member of the [Windrush generation](/wiki/Windrush_generation "Windrush generation").[[64]](#cite_note-64) The plaque was launched with campaigners including [Patrick Vernon](/wiki/Patrick_Vernon "Patrick Vernon") and [Claire Darke](/wiki/Claire_Darke "Claire Darke") at the Wolverhampton Heritage Centre.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The centre is a cornerstone of the area's local Caribbean community and was formerly the constituency office of [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell") where his [Rivers of Blood speech](/wiki/Rivers_of_Blood_speech "Rivers of Blood speech") was written.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

#### Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government")]

On 20 February 2021, it was announced as part of the government's levelling up strategy, that the [Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities](/wiki/Department_for_Levelling_Up,_Housing_and_Communities "Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities") (now the [Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government](/wiki/Ministry_of_Housing,_Communities_and_Local_Government "Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government")) would be the first government department to have a headquarters based outside of London. Five hundred posts, including those of senior civil servants, are scheduled to be moving to Wolverhampton by 2025.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The then Secretary of State, [Robert Jenrick](/wiki/Robert_Jenrick "Robert Jenrick") officially opened the Ministry's new offices in the i9 building at the city's public transport interchange development on 10 September 2021.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Art and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Art and culture")]

From the 18th century, Wolverhampton was well known for production of [japanned ware](/wiki/Japanned_ware "Japanned ware") and steel jewellery. The renowned 18th- and 19th-century artists [Joseph Barney](/wiki/Joseph_Barney "Joseph Barney") (1753–1832), [Edward Bird](/wiki/Edward_Bird "Edward Bird") (1772–1819), and [George Wallis](/wiki/George_Wallis "George Wallis") (1811–1891) were all born in Wolverhampton and initially trained as japanned ware painters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The School of Practical Art was opened in the 1850s and eventually became a close associate of the Art Gallery. Among its students and teachers were Robert Jackson Emerson (1878–1944), Sir Charles Wheeler (Emerson's most famous pupil and the sculptor of the fountains in [Trafalgar Square](/wiki/Trafalgar_Square "Trafalgar Square")), [Sara Page](/wiki/Sara_Page "Sara Page") who established her studio in Paris, and many other artists and sculptors recognised locally and nationally.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Wolverhampton Art Gallery](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Art_Gallery "Wolverhampton Art Gallery") was established in 1884, whilst [Wolverhampton Grand Theatre](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Grand_Theatre "Wolverhampton Grand Theatre") was opened in 1894.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There is a Creative Industries Quarter in Wolverhampton, just off Broad Street, with facilities ranging from the newly opened [Slade Rooms](/w/index.php?title=Slade_Rooms&action=edit&redlink=1 "Slade Rooms (page does not exist)"), to the art house cinema the [Light House Media Centre](/wiki/Light_House_Media_Centre "Light House Media Centre") (closed in 2022) and the Arena Theatre, which is part of the [University of Wolverhampton](/wiki/University_of_Wolverhampton "University of Wolverhampton").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Wolverhampton has a strong history in the ornate cast iron safe painting industry from the Victorian era. Numerous companies, such as [Chubb Lock and Safe Company](/wiki/Chubb_Locks "Chubb Locks"), expanded their artistic status to international reputation, whereby a safe became a work of art with fine script and hand-painted designs. The Chubb Building was converted into a National Historic Registered Landmark Treasure in 1992, which now houses a cinema, art galleries, nightclub, business offices and a large stained glass [rotunda](/wiki/Rotunda_(architecture) "Rotunda (architecture)") in its foyer. It is among the few canal street factories in the "Black Country" that has been preserved.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Wolverhampton's biggest public art display took place between July and September 2017; [Wolves in Wolves](/wiki/Wolves_in_Wolves "Wolves in Wolves") saw the installation of 30 wolf sculptures in the city centre and West Park, with the sculptures auctioned off to raise money for charity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Exhibitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Exhibitions")]

As its wealth and influence grew, Wolverhampton both took part in notable exhibitions and hosted them. [The Great Exhibition](/wiki/The_Great_Exhibition "The Great Exhibition") of 1851, at [The Crystal Palace](/wiki/The_Crystal_Palace "The Crystal Palace"), had examples of locks, [japanned ware](/wiki/Japanning#Wolverhampton_and_Bilston "Japanning"), [enamel ware](/wiki/Vitreous_enamel "Vitreous enamel") and [papier-mâché](/wiki/Papier-m%C3%A2ch%C3%A9#Europe "Papier-mâché") products all manufactured in Wolverhampton.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Following successful exhibitions at Mechanics' Institutes in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") and many northern towns, Wolverhampton held an exhibition that was the brain child of [George Wallis](/wiki/George_Wallis "George Wallis"), an artist employed by the firm of Ryton and Walton. The exhibition was held in the Mechanics' Institute in Queen Street and showed fine art, furniture, and decorated trays, as well as a variety of ironwork, locks and steel toys.[[70]](#cite_note-Exhibitions-70)

On 11 May 1869 [The Earl Granville](/wiki/Granville_Leveson-Gower,_2nd_Earl_Granville "Granville Leveson-Gower, 2nd Earl Granville") opened the Exhibition of Staffordshire Arts and Industry in a temporary building in the grounds of Molineux House.[[70]](#cite_note-Exhibitions-70)

The largest and most ambitious exhibition was the Arts and Industrial Exhibition which took place in 1902. Although housing only one international pavilion, from Canada, the scope and scale of the exhibition mirrored all the advances in other exhibitions of its time. The exhibition site featured several halls housing machinery, industrial products, a concert hall, two bandstands, a restaurant, and a fun fair with thrill rides and a water chute. Its opening, by the [Duke of Connaught](/wiki/Duke_of_Connaught "Duke of Connaught"), was received with hopeful enthusiasm, unfortunately not matched by the weather, which contributed to a £30,000 loss, equivalent to nearly £2M at today's value.[[70]](#cite_note-Exhibitions-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

Wolverhampton lies 13 miles (21 km) northwest of its larger near-neighbour [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), and forms the second largest part of the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation").[[72]](#cite_note-72) To the north and west lies the [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") and [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") countryside.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Wolverhampton city centre falls outside of the area traditionally known as the [Black Country](/wiki/Black_Country "Black Country"), although some areas such as the town and former borough of [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston") and [Heath Town](/wiki/Heath_Town "Heath Town") and the [Willenhall](/wiki/Willenhall "Willenhall") side of Wolverhampton fall within the Black Country [coalfields](/wiki/Coalfield "Coalfield"), leading to confusion as to whether the entire city falls within the region. Modern usage has tended towards using the term to refer to the western part of the [West Midlands county](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)"), excluding Birmingham, [Solihull](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Solihull "Metropolitan Borough of Solihull") and [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"). Examples would be UK Government regional bodies such as the [Black Country Development Corporation](/wiki/Black_Country_Development_Corporation "Black Country Development Corporation"), under whose remit the city fell.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The city lies upon the [Midlands Plateau](/wiki/Midlands_Plateau "Midlands Plateau") at 163 metres (535 ft) above sea level.[[73]](#cite_note-73) There are no major rivers within the city, although the [River Penk](/wiki/River_Penk "River Penk") and [River Tame](/wiki/River_Tame,_West_Midlands "River Tame, West Midlands") (tributaries of the [River Trent](/wiki/River_Trent "River Trent")) rise in the city, as does [Smestow Brook](/wiki/Smestow_Brook "Smestow Brook"), a tributary of the [River Stour](/wiki/River_Stour,_Worcestershire "River Stour, Worcestershire"), and thence the [River Severn](/wiki/River_Severn "River Severn"). This means that the city lies astride the main east–west [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") of England.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The geology of the city is complex, with a combination of [Triassic](/wiki/Triassic "Triassic") and [Carboniferous](/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") geology; specifically [Bunter](/wiki/Bunter_(geology) "Bunter (geology)") and [Keuper](/wiki/Keuper "Keuper") [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), and Upper and Middle [Coal measures](/wiki/Coal_measures "Coal measures"). There is also an area of [dolerite](/wiki/Diabase "Diabase") intrusions.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Wolverhampton's climate is [oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfb*) and therefore quite temperate, with average maximum temperatures in July being around 21 °C (70 °F), and with the maximum daytime temperature in January being around 6.9 °C (44.4 °F).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Met Office](/wiki/Met_Office "Met Office")'s nearest [observation station](/wiki/Penkridge_weather_station "Penkridge weather station") is at [Penkridge](/wiki/Penkridge "Penkridge"), about 10 miles (16 km) north of the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Green belt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Green belt")]

Main article: [West Midlands Green Belt](/wiki/West_Midlands_Green_Belt "West Midlands Green Belt")

Wolverhampton has [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt "Green belt") within its boundary, as a part of the wider West Midlands Green Belt. This is scattered around the western half of the city, in the form of [green wedges](/wiki/Green_belt_(United_Kingdom)#Environmental "Green belt (United Kingdom)"), due to it being highly urbanised.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The green belt is in place to prevent further [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"), and preserve greenfield areas. Areas covered include:[[79]](#cite_note-79)

* Moseley Parklands
* Land by Grassy Lane at Wood Hayes
* Ashmore Park
* Goldthorn/Lower Penn Green
* Holy Trinity Church, Ettingshall Park
* The Grange, Wergs
* Beacon Hill Cemetery
* Land south of Pattingham
* Aldersley School
* Perton Road/Boundary Farm
* South Staffs Golf Course
* Highfields School
* Pennwood Lane
* Smestow Valley/Valley Park
* Goldthorn/Lower Penn Green

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Government")]

The vast majority of Wolverhampton is governed locally by City of Wolverhampton Council, although some smaller parts of the urban area are governed by [South Staffordshire](/wiki/South_Staffordshire "South Staffordshire") District Council.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The area administered by the City Council is represented in the national United Kingdom parliament by three MPs representing [Wolverhampton West](/wiki/Wolverhampton_West_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wolverhampton West (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Wolverhampton South East](/wiki/Wolverhampton_South_East "Wolverhampton South East") and [Wolverhampton North East](/wiki/Wolverhampton_North_East "Wolverhampton North East") constituencies, with the areas administered by South Staffordshire District Council being represented by [South Staffordshire constituency](/wiki/South_Staffordshire_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South Staffordshire (UK Parliament constituency)"). The entire city was part of the [West Midlands constituency](/wiki/West_Midlands_(European_Parliament_constituency) "West Midlands (European Parliament constituency)") of the European Parliament.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### City of Wolverhampton Council

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: City of Wolverhampton Council")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Civic_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_447471.jpg/250px-Civic_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_447471.jpg)](/wiki/File:Civic_Centre_-_geograph.org.uk_-_447471.jpg)

[Wolverhampton Civic Centre](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Civic_Centre "Wolverhampton Civic Centre")

Main article: [City of Wolverhampton Council](/wiki/City_of_Wolverhampton_Council "City of Wolverhampton Council")

The City of Wolverhampton is a [metropolitan borough](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough"), with its City Council acting as a single-tier [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"). South Staffordshire District Council is a two-tier authority, with some services provided by [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") County Council.
The council offices are in the [Wolverhampton Civic Centre](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Civic_Centre "Wolverhampton Civic Centre"), which is located in St Peter's Square in the city centre.[[80]](#cite_note-80) The city council's motto is 'Out of darkness cometh light'.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") currently control the council and have been in majority on the council since 1974, with the exceptions of 1978–1979, 1987, 1992–1994 and 2008–2010.[[82]](#cite_note-council_stats-82) The Labour party won 47 out of 60 council seats in [2023](/wiki/2023_City_of_Wolverhampton_Council_election "2023 City of Wolverhampton Council election"), the last year all the seats were up for election.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

See also: [List of Mayors of Wolverhampton](/wiki/List_of_Mayors_of_Wolverhampton "List of Mayors of Wolverhampton")

### Civic history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Civic history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Wolverhampton_1921.jpg/250px-Wolverhampton_1921.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wolverhampton_1921.jpg)

Wolverhampton in 1921

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Town_Hall_%2C_North_St._%2C_Wolverhampton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_538393.jpg/250px-Town_Hall_%2C_North_St._%2C_Wolverhampton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_538393.jpg)](/wiki/File:Town_Hall_,_North_St._,_Wolverhampton_-_geograph.org.uk_-_538393.jpg)

[The old Town Hall](/wiki/Old_Town_Hall,_Wolverhampton "Old Town Hall, Wolverhampton") (magistrates court)

Wolverhampton gained the beginnings of modern local government in 1777, when the Wolverhampton Improvement Act was passed by Parliament. This allowed for the establishment of 125 Town Commissioners who undertook a variety of local improvement work such as punishing bear baiting, improving drainage, widening streets and by the end of the century street lighting had been provided at every street corner and over the doorway of every inn, and water supply had been improved by the sinking of ten new wells and the provision of a great water tank in the market place. Policing had been improved with the appointment of ten watchmen and attempts were also made to regulate the markets and inspect hazardous food.[[4]](#cite_note-farley-4)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

[Wolverhampton parliamentary borough](/wiki/Wolverhampton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wolverhampton (UK Parliament constituency)") was created by the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"), which included areas currently located with the Metropolitan Boroughs of [Dudley](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Dudley "Metropolitan Borough of Dudley"), [Walsall](/wiki/Metropolitan_Borough_of_Walsall "Metropolitan Borough of Walsall") and [Sandwell](/wiki/Sandwell "Sandwell") such as [Wren's Nest](/wiki/Wren%27s_Nest "Wren's Nest"), [New Invention](/wiki/New_Invention,_Walsall "New Invention, Walsall") and [Sedgley](/wiki/Sedgley "Sedgley"). It was one of 22 large towns that returned two members of parliament. Under the [Redistribution of Seats Act 1885](/wiki/Redistribution_of_Seats_Act_1885 "Redistribution of Seats Act 1885"), the original borough was replaced by three new single-member constituencies: [Wolverhampton East](/wiki/Wolverhampton_East_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wolverhampton East (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Wolverhampton South](/wiki/Wolverhampton_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wolverhampton South (UK Parliament constituency)") and [Wolverhampton West](/wiki/Wolverhampton_West_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wolverhampton West (UK Parliament constituency)").[[85]](#cite_note-85)

In 1837, [Wolverhampton Borough Police](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Borough_Police "Wolverhampton Borough Police") was formed. It was disestablished in 1966, and the larger [West Midlands Constabulary](/wiki/West_Midlands_Constabulary "West Midlands Constabulary"), which covered not only Wolverhampton but the County Boroughs of [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall"), [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley"), [West Bromwich](/wiki/West_Bromwich "West Bromwich") and [Warley](/wiki/County_Borough_of_Warley "County Borough of Warley") took over its duties and was headquartered in the city. This force was then replaced in 1974 with the West Midlands Police.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Wolverhampton was incorporated as a [municipal borough](/wiki/Municipal_borough "Municipal borough") in 1848 under the [Municipal Corporations Act 1835](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_Act_1835 "Municipal Corporations Act 1835").[[87]](#cite_note-StaffsArchive-87) and the first meeting of the Council took place on 22 May 1848.[[88]](#cite_note-88) The town was then made a [County Borough](/wiki/County_Borough "County Borough") in 1889 under the [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888").[[87]](#cite_note-StaffsArchive-87)

In 1933, the boundaries of the borough expanded, taking in areas from [Cannock Rural District](/wiki/Cannock_Rural_District "Cannock Rural District") and [Seisdon Rural District](/wiki/Seisdon_Rural_District "Seisdon Rural District"), with very little of the surrounding urban area being affected,[[89]](#cite_note-avob-89) with only [Heath Town](/wiki/Heath_Town "Heath Town") Urban District being abolished.

The bulk of the [urban districts](/wiki/Urban_district_(Great_Britain_and_Ireland) "Urban district (Great Britain and Ireland)") of Bilston (a borough itself after 1933), [Tettenhall](/wiki/Tettenhall "Tettenhall") and [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield") were added to the borough in 1966, along with the northern section of the urban district of [Coseley](/wiki/Coseley "Coseley") and parts from the north of [Sedgley](/wiki/Sedgley "Sedgley") and the west of [Willenhall](/wiki/Willenhall "Willenhall"). The vast majority of these areas were traditionally part of the Parish of Wolverhampton, and were part of the original Parliamentary Borough.[[89]](#cite_note-avob-89)

Wolverhampton was one of only two County Boroughs (the other being [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool")) to have no changes made to the boundary during the [1974 reorganisation of local government](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), the borough already having a population larger than the 250,000 required for education authorities. This contrasted with both the [Redcliffe-Maud Report](/wiki/Redcliffe-Maud_Report "Redcliffe-Maud Report"), and the initial White Paper for the 1974 reforms[[90]](#cite_note-white_paper-90) where large areas of the present [South Staffordshire](/wiki/South_Staffordshire "South Staffordshire") district were to be added to the borough. During the 1974 reforms it was placed within the [West Midlands Metropolitan County](/wiki/West_Midlands_(county) "West Midlands (county)").

Wolverhampton was also a [Royal Peculiar](/wiki/Royal_Peculiar "Royal Peculiar") covering a large area.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### Police

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Police")]

The main police station for Wolverhampton is based on Bilston Street[[92]](#cite_note-WMP_YLS-92) in the city centre. [Wolverhampton Borough Police](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Borough_Police "Wolverhampton Borough Police") became part of [West Midlands Constabulary](/wiki/West_Midlands_Constabulary "West Midlands Constabulary") in 1966.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Policing is currently delivered by West Midlands Police.[[92]](#cite_note-WMP_YLS-92)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Wolverhampton_pop_pyramid.svg/250px-Wolverhampton_pop_pyramid.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wolverhampton_pop_pyramid.svg)

Population pyramid of Wolverhampton (borough) in 2021

The [2021 Census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Census_2021 "United Kingdom Census 2021") gives the Wolverhampton Urban Subdivision as the second largest in the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation"). The figure given for Wolverhampton is 263,727. The proportion of females within the city (50.9%) is slightly higher than that of males (49.1%).[[94]](#cite_note-Nomis22-94)

Wolverhampton has an ethnically diverse population; 60.6% of the city's population were white in the 2021 census, 21.2% were [Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian"), 9.3% were [Black](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people"), 5.3% were [mixed](/wiki/Mixed_race "Mixed race"), and 3.6 were from another ethnic group.[[94]](#cite_note-Nomis22-94)

Based upon the 2021 census, Wolverhampton has a Christian population of 43.8%. The proportion of Wolverhampton residents professing [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") was 27.8%. Of religious groupings, [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") has the second largest following in Wolverhampton, accounting for 12% of the population in 2021, increasing from 9.1% in the 2011 census, meaning the city has the [largest percentage](/wiki/Sikhism_in_the_United_Kingdom#Geographical_distribution "Sikhism in the United Kingdom") of Sikhs in England and Wales, and the third largest community numerically. The proportion of people following [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") was 5.5%, whilst [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") was followed by 3.7%, the figure for [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") was 0.3%.[[94]](#cite_note-Nomis22-94)

Wolverhampton is within the top 11% of local council areas in England and Wales (excluding London Boroughs) for public transport use for travelling to work at 16% of the total. 63% used private transport, either as a driver or passenger, 13% cycled or travelled on foot, whilst 8% worked from home.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

Car ownership is lower than the average for England and Wales with 35.2% of households not owning a car, compared with 26.8% nationally. Single car ownership is in line with national averages (Wolverhampton 42.9%, England and Wales 43.8%), while the proportion of households owning more than one car is lower than the national average.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

As of the [2021 United Kingdom census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"), with Wolverhampton's high [British Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian") population, the [British Muslim](/wiki/British_Muslim "British Muslim"), [British Hindus](/wiki/British_hindus "British hindus") and Sikh communities are more numerous than the national average.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Partial_view_of_the_Mills_of_Thakurgaon_Sugar_Mills_%286%29_%2802.03.2019%29.jpg/250px-Partial_view_of_the_Mills_of_Thakurgaon_Sugar_Mills_%286%29_%2802.03.2019%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Partial_view_of_the_Mills_of_Thakurgaon_Sugar_Mills_(6)_(02.03.2019).jpg)

Water-tube boiler made in Wolverhampton

Traditionally, Wolverhampton's economy has been dominated by iron, steel, [automobiles](/wiki/Automobiles "Automobiles"), engineering and manufacturing industries. Many of the traditional industries in the city have closed or dramatically downsized over the years. However, by 2008 the economy was dominated by the [service sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_economic_activity "Tertiary sector of economic activity"),[[118]](#cite_note-118) with 74.9% of the city's employment being in this area. The major subcomponents of this sector are in public administration, education and health (32.8% of the total employment), while distribution, hotels and restaurants take up 21.1%, and finance and IT takes up 12.7%. The largest non-service industry was that of manufacturing (12.9%), whilst 5.2% of the total employment is related to the tourism industry.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

The largest single employer within the city is Wolverhampton City Council.[[120]](#cite_note-120) which has over 12,000 staff[[121]](#cite_note-121) Other large employers within the city include:

* Banking: [Birmingham Midshires](/wiki/Birmingham_Midshires "Birmingham Midshires") (Headquarters)
* Building materials: [Tarmac](/wiki/Tarmac_Group "Tarmac Group") and Carvers Builders Merchant
* Education: [University of Wolverhampton](/wiki/University_of_Wolverhampton "University of Wolverhampton") and [City of Wolverhampton College](/wiki/City_of_Wolverhampton_College "City of Wolverhampton College")
* Construction: [Carillion](/wiki/Carillion "Carillion") (Headquarters). Entered liquidation January 2018
* Brewing: [Marston's](/wiki/Marston%27s "Marston's") (Headquarters)
* Aerospace: H S Marston, [MOOG](/wiki/Moog_Inc "Moog Inc") and [Goodrich Actuation Systems](/wiki/Goodrich_Corporation "Goodrich Corporation")
* Retail: [Beatties](/wiki/Beatties "Beatties") (bought by [House of Fraser](/wiki/House_of_Fraser "House of Fraser") 2005, closed 2019[[62]](#cite_note-eas20180607-62))
* Manufacturing: [Chubb Locks](/wiki/Chubb_Locks "Chubb Locks"), [Jaguar Land Rover](/wiki/Jaguar_Land_Rover "Jaguar Land Rover") ([Engine Assembly Plant](/wiki/Engine_Manufacturing_Centre "Engine Manufacturing Centre"))
* National Health Service (NHS): [New Cross Hospital](/wiki/New_Cross_Hospital "New Cross Hospital")

### Jaguar Land Rover

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Jaguar Land Rover")]

In 2014 [Jaguar Land Rover](/wiki/Jaguar_Land_Rover "Jaguar Land Rover") opened a £500 million [Engine Assembly Plant](/wiki/Engine_Manufacturing_Centre "Engine Manufacturing Centre") at the [i54](/wiki/I54 "I54") business park, Wolverhampton. Unveiled by [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), the plant produces 2.0-litre 4-cylinder Ingenium diesel and petrol engines. Having already been expanded once before, in 2015 it was announced that the factory would be doubling in size to 200,000 sq m (2,152,782 sq ft), costing $450 million.[[122]](#cite_note-122) This expansion would see the workforce double from 700 to 1,400.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

### Moog Aircraft Group

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Moog Aircraft Group")]

[Moog Aircraft Group](/wiki/Moog_Inc. "Moog Inc.") operates an aerospace manufacturing facility on Valiant Way in the [Pendeford](/wiki/Pendeford "Pendeford") area of Wolverhampton.[[124]](#cite_note-source1-124)[[125]](#cite_note-source1a-125)The site forms part of the city’s advanced manufacturing sector and produces flight control systems for aircraft including the [Boeing 777](/wiki/Boeing_777 "Boeing 777"), [Boeing 787](/wiki/Boeing_787 "Boeing 787"), [Airbus A330](/wiki/Airbus_A330 "Airbus A330") and [Airbus A380](/wiki/Airbus_A380 "Airbus A380").[[126]](#cite_note-source2-126) The facility employs several hundred staff.[[127]](#cite_note-source3-127)The Moog site received media attention in August 2025 when four pro-Palestinian activists were arrested after breaking into the facility and setting off flares, causing over £1 million in damage.[[128]](#cite_note-source4-128)[[129]](#cite_note-source4a-129) The incident required police involvement to secure the manufacturing facility and staff, while legal proceedings against the activists were initiated at the [Old Bailey](/wiki/Old_Bailey "Old Bailey") and [Birmingham Crown Court](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II_Law_Courts,_Birmingham "Queen Elizabeth II Law Courts, Birmingham").[[129]](#cite_note-source4a-129)

### Goodyear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Goodyear")]

[Goodyear](/wiki/Goodyear_Tire_and_Rubber_Company "Goodyear Tire and Rubber Company") opened a large factory on Stafford Road, [Fordhouses](/wiki/Fordhouses "Fordhouses"), in 1927. In December 2003, it was announced car tyre production at the plant would be discontinued with the loss of more than 400 jobs. This came after some 2,000 job losses at the plant since 1997.[[130]](#cite_note-130) The factory remained open for tyre moulding and tractor tyre production. The factory ceased production at the end of 2016 and closed for good in 2017.[[131]](#cite_note-131) The factory buildings were demolished in 2018, with only the clock tower retained.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

### Wolverhampton Wanderers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Wolverhampton Wanderers")]

The varied success of [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") since the club's formation in 1877 has also contributed to the economy of Wolverhampton. The club's greatest successes came between 1949 and 1960, when it won the league title three times and the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") twice, as well as hosting a series of high-profile friendly matches against Europe's leading club sides, also competing in two early editions of the [European Cup](/wiki/European_Cup "European Cup"). During this era, Wolves frequently attracted crowds of between 40,000 and 50,000. Wolves went on to win the [Football League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") in 1974 and again in 1980, spending all but three seasons outside the top division of English football during the first 35 years of postwar league football, and continued to attract strong crowds but the 1980s saw a sharp decline in the club's fortunes, with three consecutive relegations, a sharp fall in attendances, and debts of more than £2million which almost put the club out of business. This decline also came at a time when the economy of Wolverhampton was struggling due to the [early 1980s recession](/wiki/Early_1980s_recession "Early 1980s recession"). Wolves enjoyed a surge in the late 1980s with two consecutive promotions, thanks largely to the prolific scoring of striker [Steve Bull](/wiki/Steve_Bull "Steve Bull"), and an upturn in attendances which also provided a boost to the local economy – as did the club's increased support during the 1990s, during which time the stadium was extensively rebuilt and its facilities improved, with its new features including a restaurant and office space. In 2018, Wolves won promotion to the [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") for the third time in 15 years, and a year later reached its first [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") semi-final since 1998, and further redevelopment of the stadium is planned.
