### Regeneration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Regeneration")]

In recent years, Wolverhampton City Council have embarked on many city improvements and regeneration schemes.[[134]](#cite_note-134) One such project was "Summer Row", a new £300 million retail quarter for Wolverhampton city centre. The project would have involved clearing existing buildings, and in 2006 a [compulsory purchase order](/wiki/Compulsory_purchase_order "Compulsory purchase order") was issued to over 200 owner / occupiers in the surrounding area.[[135]](#cite_note-135) Construction of Summer Row was originally earmarked for 2008, with a completion date listed as 2010,[[136]](#cite_note-136) but the 2008 recession put the project on hold.
In January 2011, the Summer Row project was formally cancelled[[137]](#cite_note-137) as the CPO expired before the council found the necessary financial backing for the project.

#### Mander Centre Redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Mander Centre Redevelopment")]

[Debenhams](/wiki/Debenhams "Debenhams"), who were listed as the anchor store of Summer Row, announced they were still keen in opening a department store in Wolverhampton. It was revealed they would open an anchor store in a £35 million redevelopment of the [Mander Centre](/wiki/Mander_Centre "Mander Centre"). To be completed in 2017, the 90,000-square-foot (8,400 m2) store would create 120 jobs.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The redevelopment would also see the Mander Centre be fully refurbished and reconfigured. A number of larger stores would be created, replacing smaller ones.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Wolverhampton Interchange Project

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Wolverhampton Interchange Project")]

Wolverhampton's Interchange Project is a major redevelopment of the city's east side area worth around £120 million.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Wolverhampton_i10.JPG/250px-Wolverhampton_i10.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wolverhampton_i10.JPG)

The i10 building contains 12,400 sq ft (1,150 m2) of leisure and retail space on the ground floor and 36,000 sq ft (3,300 m2) of office space above

* Phase 1, which was completed in 2012, consisted of demolishing the old bus station and replacing it with a new £22.5 million station. This phase also included a new footbridge across the ring road towards the railway station, highway and pedestrian works, and new offices for [Centro](/wiki/Transport_for_West_Midlands "Transport for West Midlands").[[139]](#cite_note-139)
* Phase 2 which was completed in late 2015, involved the construction of the £10.6 million i10 building adjacent to the new bus station. The building contains 12,400 square feet (1,150 m2) of leisure and retail space on the ground floor and 36,000 sq ft (3,300 m2) of office space above.[[140]](#cite_note-140)
* Phase 3 began in early 2016 on expanding the train station's [multi-storey car park](/wiki/Multi-storey_car_park "Multi-storey car park"). Completed in December 2016, the car park increased in capacity from 450 to over 800 spaces.[[141]](#cite_note-141) The expansion of the multi-storey car park included a new cycle and motorcycle parking, short stay parking, passenger drop off point and a taxi rank adjacent to the car park. A new entrance was created.[[142]](#cite_note-142)
* Phase 4 involved construction of a new development on the opposite side of Railway Drive to the i10 and is known as i9. The offices are used by the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government, part of central government.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Road")]

Wolverhampton city centre forms the main focal point for the road network within the northwestern part of the [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation"), and out into the rural hinterland of [Staffordshire](/wiki/Staffordshire "Staffordshire") and [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"). The road network within the boundaries of the city council area is entirely maintained by [Wolverhampton City Council](/wiki/Wolverhampton_City_Council "Wolverhampton City Council"), whilst those parts of the urban area outside the city council area have their networks maintained by [Staffordshire County Council](/wiki/Staffordshire_County_Council "Staffordshire County Council"), with the exception of [M54](/wiki/M54_motorway "M54 motorway") and [A449](/wiki/A449_road "A449 road") on the northern fringes of the urban area which are maintained by [National Highways](/wiki/National_Highways "National Highways").[[143]](#cite_note-highwaysagency-143)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Wolverhampton_Ring_Road.JPG/250px-Wolverhampton_Ring_Road.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wolverhampton_Ring_Road.JPG)

Wolverhampton's Ring Road

Major historical improvements to the city's road network include [Thomas Telford](/wiki/Thomas_Telford "Thomas Telford")'s Holyhead Road (now part of [A41](/wiki/A41_road "A41 road")), which was constructed between 1819 and 1826 to improve communications between London and [Holyhead](/wiki/Holyhead "Holyhead"), and hence to Ireland. The majority of work within the city saw improvement to the contemporary network, though both the Wellington Road in [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston")[[144]](#cite_note-144) and the cutting at the Rock near [Tettenhall](/wiki/Tettenhall "Tettenhall") were newly constructed for the road, although the improvements at The Rock were constructed by the local Turnpike Trust rather than Telford himself.[[145]](#cite_note-145) In 1927, the [A4123](/wiki/A4123_road "A4123 road") [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham")-Wolverhampton New Road was constructed as both an unemployment relief project, and to relieve pressure on Telford's road through the [Black Country](/wiki/Black_Country "Black Country").[[146]](#cite_note-146) It was the first purpose built inter-city road in the United Kingdom within the 20th century,[[147]](#cite_note-147) and was said to be the longest stretch of new road in Britain since the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). It took just three years to complete and cost £600,000.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Also in 1927, the first automatic [traffic lights](/wiki/Traffic_light "Traffic light") in the United Kingdom were installed in Princes Square in the city centre.[[39]](#cite_note-Wolverhampton_City_Council-39) Princes Square was also the location of the United Kingdom's first pedestrian safety barriers, which were erected in 1934.[[40]](#cite_note-Steelway_Limited-40)

In 1960, plans were announced to build a [Ring Road](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Ring_Road "Wolverhampton Ring Road") around the centre of Wolverhampton. By the end of the 1960s, more than half of the Ring Road had been completed, stretching from Snow Hill to Stafford Street (via Penn Road, Chapel Ash and Waterloo Road), followed a few years later by a section between Snow Hill and Bilston Street. However, the final section between Bilston Street and Stafford Street (via Wednesfield Road) was not completed until 1986. An outer ring road was also planned but only a small section was ever built, this being Wobaston Road in Pendeford.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/M54Motorway2.jpg/250px-M54Motorway2.jpg)](/wiki/File:M54Motorway2.jpg)

The M54 motorway to the northwest of the city

Wolverhampton is near to several motorways, with four being located within 7 miles (11 km) of the city centre. The first to be constructed in the area was the [M6](/wiki/M6_motorway "M6 motorway"), which opened in sections between 1966 and 1970,[[149]](#cite_note-tma-149) and connects the city with the north-west of England (including [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool")), Scotland as well as [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") and [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry") to the east, and London via the [M1](/wiki/M1_motorway "M1 motorway"). Together with the [M5](/wiki/M5_motorway "M5 motorway"), which opened in the area in 1970[[149]](#cite_note-tma-149) and links the city with the south-west of England, and London via the [M40](/wiki/M40_motorway "M40 motorway"), the two motorways form a north–south bypass for the city.

The section of M6 motorway nearest to the city is one of the busiest within the UK,[[150]](#cite_note-150) and to relieve congestion on this stretch, the [M6 Toll](/wiki/M6_Toll "M6 Toll") which bypasses both the Wolverhampton and [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") sections of the M6 motorway was opened in 2003.[[149]](#cite_note-tma-149)

The [M54 motorway](/wiki/M54_motorway "M54 motorway") forms a northern bypass to the city, passing just within the fringes of the urban area, and links the city with [Telford](/wiki/Telford "Telford"), [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury "Shrewsbury") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). It opened in 1983.[[149]](#cite_note-tma-149)

In addition to the motorways presently constructed, there have also been several proposed near to the city that have not been constructed, or have been constructed to a lower standard. Included within these are the *Bilston Link Motorway*, which was first proposed in the 1960s and was eventually constructed to a lower standard in the 1980s as the [A454](/wiki/A454_road "A454 road")/[A463](/wiki/A463_road "A463 road") [Black Country Route](/wiki/Black_Country_Route "Black Country Route");[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and the *Western Orbital* or *Wolverhampton Western Bypass*, which was first proposed in the 1970s as a bypass for the western side of the city and the wider [West Midlands conurbation](/wiki/West_Midlands_conurbation "West Midlands conurbation").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Currently proposed by the [Highways Agency](/wiki/Highways_Agency "Highways Agency") is the *M54 to M6 / M6 (Toll) Link Road*. The route was initially proposed in the 2000s to relieve the overloaded sections of [A460](/wiki/A460_road "A460 road") and [A449](/wiki/A449_road "A449 road") near the city, and to replace a section of the cancelled Western Orbital. Whilst it appears in the current roads programme, a date for the start of construction has not been set.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Rail")]

Wolverhampton is well connected by rail to other parts of England and Wales. However, while Priestfield and Bilston are connected by a tram line, the suburbs in the south, west and north of Wolverhampton are no longer served by rail - in particular the Tettenhall, Penn and Compton areas and Dunstall Park for the race course. Wednesfield no longer has any rail connections, with [Wolverhampton](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_station "Wolverhampton railway station") and [Bloxwich](/wiki/Bloxwich_railway_station "Bloxwich railway station") being its closest railway stations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Wolverhampton's first railway opened in 1837, with the opening of the [Grand Junction Railway](/wiki/Grand_Junction_Railway "Grand Junction Railway"), the first long-distance line in Great Britain. The main station for the city was, however, not located in the city centre but at [Wednesfield Heath](/wiki/Wednesfield_Heath_railway_station "Wednesfield Heath railway station"), now [Heath Town](/wiki/Heath_Town "Heath Town") on the east side of the city.[[24]](#cite_note-Virgin_Trains-24) This station was considered to be a First Class station, though its location was obviously not ideal and it became a goods station after passenger services ceased in 1873. The station buildings were demolished in 1965, but the main station area is now a nature reserve just off Powell Street, called Station Fields and part of the edge of the northbound platform is still in situ. The track running through the station site is, however, still in use.[[25]](#cite_note-Heath_Town_Station-25)

The [first station in the city centre](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Temporary_railway_station "Wolverhampton Temporary railway station") was opened by the [Shrewsbury and Birmingham Railway](/wiki/Shrewsbury_and_Birmingham_Railway "Shrewsbury and Birmingham Railway") in 1849. This station was only intended to be temporary and was located on the north side of Wednesfield Road, beside Broad Street Basin. The station was constructed as the opening of [Wolverhampton High Level](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_station "Wolverhampton railway station") was delayed. The station closed in 1852 and was demolished in the mid-1970s.[[152]](#cite_note-152) In addition to the temporary station, [Wolverhampton Works](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Works "Wolverhampton Works") were also established in 1849 by the Shrewsbury and Birmingham Railway and became the Northern Division workshop of the [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") in 1854.[[26]](#cite_note-manufacturing-26)

The permanent station on the line finally opened on 24 June 1852 and was initially known as *Wolverhampton General*; it was renamed as *Wolverhampton Queen Street* in 1853 and finally [Wolverhampton High Level](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_station "Wolverhampton railway station") in 1855. The station was initially a joint station between the [Shrewsbury and Birmingham Railway](/wiki/Shrewsbury_and_Birmingham_Railway "Shrewsbury and Birmingham Railway") and the [London and North Western Railway](/wiki/London_and_North_Western_Railway "London and North Western Railway"), though there were problems in the relationships between the two companies; the station became solely LNWR in 1854, before the [Wolverhampton and Walsall Railway](/wiki/Wolverhampton_and_Walsall_Railway "Wolverhampton and Walsall Railway") (later part of the [Midland Railway](/wiki/Midland_Railway "Midland Railway")) gained access to the station in 1867. The original High Level station was demolished in 1965, as part of the electification of the [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line") and was replaced by buildings on the present site which have now been demolished.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

Two years after the opening of the High Level station, the [Oxford, Worcester and Wolverhampton Railway](/wiki/Oxford,_Worcester_and_Wolverhampton_Railway "Oxford, Worcester and Wolverhampton Railway") (OWW) opened their city centre station immediately to the east of High Level. Initially called *Wolverhampton Joint*, it was renamed [Wolverhampton Low Level](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Low_Level_railway_station "Wolverhampton Low Level railway station") in 1856. As well as the OWW, the station also served the [Birmingham, Wolverhampton and Dudley Railway](/wiki/Birmingham,_Wolverhampton_and_Dudley_Railway "Birmingham, Wolverhampton and Dudley Railway") and the Shrewsbury and Birmingham Railway. As the first two companies were supported by the [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway"), [broad gauge](/wiki/Broad_gauge "Broad gauge") track was laid to the station, meaning that Wolverhampton Low Level became the most northerly station on the broad gauge network before being converted to standard gauge in 1869. Despite being featured in the second [Beeching Report](/wiki/Beeching_Report "Beeching Report"), *The Development of the Major Railway Trunk Routes* in February 1965 as being on a line earmarked for further investment, services were withdrawn progressively from Low Level starting in 1967, soon after it had been transferred administratively from the [Western Region of British Railways](/wiki/Western_Region_of_British_Railways "Western Region of British Railways") to the [London Midland region](/wiki/London_Midland_Region_of_British_Railways "London Midland Region of British Railways"). London services were transferred to the newly electrified High Level station. Low Level was converted into a Parcels Concentration Depot in 1970 and the final passenger services were withdrawn in 1972.[[154]](#cite_note-154) These services (to and from [Birmingham Snow Hill](/wiki/Birmingham_Snow_Hill_railway_station "Birmingham Snow Hill railway station")) were only suspended and never legally withdrawn by [British Rail](/wiki/British_Rail "British Rail") and so technically the station is still open.[[155]](#cite_note-155)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Dunstall_Park_station_geograph-2516514-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg/250px-Dunstall_Park_station_geograph-2516514-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dunstall_Park_station_geograph-2516514-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg)

[Dunstall Park railway station](/wiki/Dunstall_Park_railway_station "Dunstall Park railway station") in 1958

There were also a number of suburban stations in Wolverhampton – including [Dunstall Park](/wiki/Dunstall_Park_railway_station "Dunstall Park railway station") and [Bushbury](/wiki/Bushbury_railway_station "Bushbury railway station") north of the city centre; [Tettenhall](/wiki/Tettenhall_railway_station "Tettenhall railway station") and [Compton](/wiki/Compton_Halt_railway_station "Compton Halt railway station") to the west side of the city on the GWR's [Wombourne Branch Line](/wiki/Wombourne_Branch_Line "Wombourne Branch Line"); [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield_railway_station "Wednesfield railway station") and [Heath Town](/wiki/Heath_Town_railway_station "Heath Town railway station") on the [Wolverhampton and Walsall Railway](/wiki/Wolverhampton_and_Walsall_Railway "Wolverhampton and Walsall Railway"); [Portobello](/wiki/Portobello_railway_station_(Wolverhampton) "Portobello railway station (Wolverhampton)") on the [Walsall to Wolverhampton Line](/wiki/Walsall_to_Wolverhampton_Line "Walsall to Wolverhampton Line"); [Priestfield](/wiki/Priestfield_railway_station "Priestfield railway station") and [Bilston Central](/wiki/Bilston_Central_railway_station "Bilston Central railway station") on the [Birmingham Snow Hill to Wolverhampton Low Level Line](/wiki/Birmingham_Snow_Hill_to_Wolverhampton_Low_Level_Line "Birmingham Snow Hill to Wolverhampton Low Level Line"); and [Bilston West](/wiki/Bilston_West_railway_station "Bilston West railway station") and [Daisy Bank](/wiki/Daisy_Bank_railway_station "Daisy Bank railway station") on the [Oxford-Worcester-Wolverhampton Line](/wiki/Oxford,_Worcester_and_Wolverhampton_Railway "Oxford, Worcester and Wolverhampton Railway"). Today, all of the suburban rail stations within the city have been closed, although [Coseley](/wiki/Coseley_railway_station "Coseley railway station"), [Codsall](/wiki/Codsall_railway_station "Codsall railway station") and [Bilbrook](/wiki/Bilbrook_railway_station "Bilbrook railway station") are just outside the boundaries. Also, some of the seven West Midlands Metro tram stations in the city are near or directly replace these former suburban stations.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Wolverhampton_Railway_Station%2C_with_new_tram_lines%2C_Ruth_Sharville%2C_geograph%2C_7411728_%28adjust%29.jpg/250px-Wolverhampton_Railway_Station%2C_with_new_tram_lines%2C_Ruth_Sharville%2C_geograph%2C_7411728_%28adjust%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wolverhampton_Railway_Station,_with_new_tram_lines,_Ruth_Sharville,_geograph,_7411728_(adjust).jpg)

[Wolverhampton railway station in 2023](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_station "Wolverhampton railway station")

The former High Level station, now simply known as [Wolverhampton station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_station "Wolverhampton railway station"), is today one of the principal stations on the [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line"). It has regular rail services to [London Euston](/wiki/Euston_railway_station "Euston railway station"), [Birmingham New Street](/wiki/Birmingham_New_Street_railway_station "Birmingham New Street railway station") and [Manchester Piccadilly](/wiki/Manchester_Piccadilly_railway_station "Manchester Piccadilly railway station"), as well as most other major cities in the UK. In addition to the long-distance services, there are many local services including those on the [Cambrian Line](/wiki/Cambrian_Line "Cambrian Line") into [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), the [Walsall to Wolverhampton Line](/wiki/Walsall_to_Wolverhampton_Line "Walsall to Wolverhampton Line") to [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_railway_station "Walsall railway station"), the [Wolverhampton to Shrewsbury Line](/wiki/Wolverhampton_to_Shrewsbury_Line "Wolverhampton to Shrewsbury Line") to [Telford](/wiki/Telford_Central_railway_station "Telford Central railway station") and [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury_railway_station "Shrewsbury railway station"); and the [Rugby-Birmingham-Stafford Line](/wiki/Rugby-Birmingham-Stafford_Line "Rugby-Birmingham-Stafford Line") to [Stafford](/wiki/Stafford_railway_station "Stafford railway station") and [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry_railway_station "Coventry railway station").[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)

The 1960s buildings of the station have now been demolished, following a lengthy delay, and are being replaced with a more modern station building. Phase 1 opened in spring 2020, which saw the creation of a new entrance. Phase 2 saw the demolition of the remainder but construction of the replacement was delayed due to the COVID-19 restrictions and opened on 28 June 2021.[[158]](#cite_note-158) This new station includes a stop on the Metro tram line which opened in September 2023.[[159]](#cite_note-159)

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Buses")]

See also: [Wolverhampton bus station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_bus_station "Wolverhampton bus station")

[National Express West Midlands](/wiki/National_Express_West_Midlands "National Express West Midlands") is the largest bus operator in the city; other operators include [Chaserider](/wiki/Chaserider "Chaserider"), [Diamond West Midlands](/wiki/Diamond_West_Midlands "Diamond West Midlands"), [Travel Express](/wiki/Travel_Express "Travel Express"), Banga Buses and [Arriva Midlands](/wiki/Arriva_Midlands "Arriva Midlands"). As well as serving suburbs, buses from the centre of Wolverhampton run to [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall"), [Telford](/wiki/Telford "Telford"), [West Bromwich](/wiki/West_Bromwich "West Bromwich"), [Stourbridge](/wiki/Stourbridge "Stourbridge"), [Cannock](/wiki/Cannock "Cannock"), [Stafford](/wiki/Stafford "Stafford"), [Sedgley](/wiki/Sedgley "Sedgley"), [Bilston](/wiki/Bilston "Bilston"), [Bloxwich](/wiki/Bloxwich "Bloxwich"), [Bridgnorth](/wiki/Bridgnorth "Bridgnorth") and [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Transport for West Midlands](/wiki/Transport_for_West_Midlands "Transport for West Midlands") operates [Wolverhampton bus station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_bus_station "Wolverhampton bus station") on Pipers Row, which was completely rebuilt in 2011.[[160]](#cite_note-160) It is situated near to the railway station, providing an interchange between bus, train and tram. National Express West Midlands has a traffic office inside the bus station where officers can monitor disruptions and make alterations if required. Colour schemes and branding have been updated following the transition from [Network West Midlands](/wiki/Transport_for_West_Midlands "Transport for West Midlands") to TfWM branding.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The previous Pipers Row bus station opened on 26 October 1986, just six years after its predecessor of 1981.[[161]](#cite_note-161) The grade II listed [Queen's Building](/wiki/Queen%27s_Building,_Wolverhampton "Queen's Building, Wolverhampton") was incorporated into the bus station as part of a further upgrade in 1990. A limited refurbishment took place in 2005–6, with new toilets and the addition of a coach stand. In 2009, plans were unveiled for a complete rebuild of the bus station, as part of Wolverhampton's Interchange Project and in April 2010 it was closed.[[162]](#cite_note-162)

### West Midlands Metro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: West Midlands Metro")]

Main article: [West Midland Metro](/wiki/West_Midland_Metro "West Midland Metro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Midland_Metro_tram_no._20_on_display_at_St._Georges%2C_Bilston_Street%2C_Wolverhampton%2C_geograph-4028311-by-P-L-Chadwick.jpg/250px-Midland_Metro_tram_no._20_on_display_at_St._Georges%2C_Bilston_Street%2C_Wolverhampton%2C_geograph-4028311-by-P-L-Chadwick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Midland_Metro_tram_no._20_on_display_at_St._Georges,_Bilston_Street,_Wolverhampton,_geograph-4028311-by-P-L-Chadwick.jpg)

[West Midlands Metro](/wiki/West_Midlands_Metro "West Midlands Metro") [CAF Urbos 3](/wiki/CAF_Urbos#CAF_Urbos_3 "CAF Urbos") trams

The West Midlands Metro, a [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") system, currently connects [Wolverhampton St George's](/wiki/Wolverhampton_St_George%27s_tram_stop "Wolverhampton St George's tram stop") to [Edgbaston Village tram stop](/wiki/Edgbaston_Village_tram_stop "Edgbaston Village tram stop") via [West Bromwich](/wiki/West_Bromwich "West Bromwich") and [Wednesbury](/wiki/Wednesbury "Wednesbury"), mostly following the former [Birmingham Snow Hill-Wolverhampton Low Level Line](/wiki/Birmingham_Snow_Hill-Wolverhampton_Low_Level_Line "Birmingham Snow Hill-Wolverhampton Low Level Line"). There were plans for further lines within the city, with both a city centre loop and a line to [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall") via [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield") and [Willenhall](/wiki/Willenhall "Willenhall"), mostly following the route of the closed [Wolverhampton and Walsall Railway](/wiki/Wolverhampton_and_Walsall_Railway "Wolverhampton and Walsall Railway"), however, these were cancelled in late 2015.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

All of the seven westernmost stations on the network are in Wolverhampton, with this number increasing to nine now Pipers Row and Wolverhampton station are connected to the network.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2014/15, Centro announced in a £40 million deal, they would be replacing the entire fleet of the 16 [AnsaldoBreda T-69](/wiki/AnsaldoBreda_T-69 "AnsaldoBreda T-69") trams with 21 [CAF Urbos 3](/wiki/CAF_Urbos#CAF_Urbos_3 "CAF Urbos") trams. The new Urbos 3 trams are 9 metres longer; at 33 metres (108 ft), with the ability of carrying 210 passengers, compared to the 156 from the T69.[[164]](#cite_note-TRE-164) Additionally with the upgraded trams, Wolverhampton's Metro Line will be expanded. As part of the Wolverhampton Interchange Project; the Metro line will be extended from [Wolverhampton St George's](/wiki/Wolverhampton_St_George%27s_tram_stop "Wolverhampton St George's tram stop") to [Wolverhampton railway station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_station "Wolverhampton railway station"), creating one stop at [Wolverhampton bus station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_bus_station "Wolverhampton bus station") and subsequently ending at the railway station. It was scheduled to open in September 2020.[[165]](#cite_note-165) However delays caused by COVID-19 mean the extension is not expected to open until September 2021. The line finally opened in the summer of 2023.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2021, construction started on a 2 new metro Lines from [Brierley Hill](/wiki/Brierley_Hill "Brierley Hill") to Wolverhampton and [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham"), one of these lines will terminate in Wolverhampton City Centre and will boost connectivity to the Black Country, this means that the corridor between Wolverhampton and Wednesbury will be served every 3 minutes by trams in peak times.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In late September 2021, funding was given for over 4 new metro projects, including a new line from Wolverhampton to [New Cross Hospital](/wiki/New_Cross_Hospital "New Cross Hospital"). This new line will extend the existing line from Wolverhampton Interchange to the hospital, via an old railway line. Another planned but not confirmed extension is from St Georges to the i54.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Air")]

Wolverhampton's original airport was at [Pendeford](/wiki/Pendeford "Pendeford"), opened in 1938 and closed on 31 December 1970.[[166]](#cite_note-166) The current [Wolverhampton Airport](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Airport "Wolverhampton Airport"), renamed from Halfpenny Green, is a small general aviation airfield located 8 miles (12.9 km) southwest of the city. Expansion of the airport has been suggested, but the current owners want to keep it as a General Aviation airfield. The airport hosts various "fly in" events and is an original World War Two airfield.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The nearest major airport is [Birmingham Airport](/wiki/Birmingham_Airport "Birmingham Airport"), approximately 25 miles (40.2 km) away. The airport is easy to reach by train, with a direct express service to it only taking on average 36 minutes. By car, it takes approximately 45 minutes via the M6 or M6 Toll Road. There are direct flights to Amritsar, Delhi, Dubai, Orlando, Dublin, Paris, Bucharest, Frankfurt and Munich[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Waterways")]

Main article: [Birmingham Canal Navigations](/wiki/Birmingham_Canal_Navigations "Birmingham Canal Navigations")

There are no navigable rivers within the city, but there are 17 mi (27 km) of navigable canals. The [Birmingham Canal Main Line](/wiki/BCN_Main_Line "BCN Main Line") passes through the city centre, connecting with the remaining portion of the [Wednesbury Oak Loop](/wiki/Wednesbury_Oak_Loop "Wednesbury Oak Loop") at Deepfields Junction, and the [Wyrley & Essington Canal](/wiki/Wyrley_%26_Essington_Canal "Wyrley & Essington Canal") at [Horseley Fields Junction](/wiki/Horseley_Fields_Junction "Horseley Fields Junction"), before passing between [the railway station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_railway_station "Wolverhampton railway station") and [the bus station](/wiki/Wolverhampton_bus_station "Wolverhampton bus station") in the city centre and then descending 132 feet (40 m) through the 21 Wolverhampton Locks and terminating at [Aldersley Junction](/wiki/Aldersley_Junction "Aldersley Junction") where it meets the [Staffordshire and Worcestershire Canal](/wiki/Staffordshire_and_Worcestershire_Canal "Staffordshire and Worcestershire Canal"), which in turn connects with the [Shropshire Union Canal](/wiki/Shropshire_Union_Canal "Shropshire Union Canal") at [Autherley Junction](/wiki/Autherley_Junction "Autherley Junction").[[167]](#cite_note-167)

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Cycling")]

Most places in the borough and some of the neighbouring villages in South Staffordshire are within easy reach of the city centre by pedal cycle and terrain is moderately hilly. Climbs tend to be of two to three minutes duration. Cycling benefits from the 20 miles per hour (32 km/h) city centre within the Ring Road and a number of routes that use quieter roads and paths to avoid the ten 'A' roads that radiate from the Ring Road. Wolverhampton is on the Smethwick to Telford section of [Sustrans](/wiki/Sustrans "Sustrans") [National Cycle Network](/wiki/National_Cycle_Network "National Cycle Network") Route 81.[[168]](#cite_note-168) This follows the [Birmingham Main Line Canal](/wiki/BCN_Main_Line "BCN Main Line") towpath from [Smethwick](/wiki/Smethwick "Smethwick") to Broad Street Basin, Wolverhampton where the route splits in two. The choice here is between riding the 21 locks section of the [Birmingham Main Line Canal](/wiki/BCN_Main_Line "BCN Main Line") to Aldersley Junction or taking the Cross-City route braid to visit the city centre, [West Park](/wiki/West_Park,_Wolverhampton "West Park, Wolverhampton") or [Smestow Valley Leisure Ride](/wiki/Smestow_Valley_Leisure_Ride "Smestow Valley Leisure Ride") before returning to Aldersley Junction. NCN81 continues to Autherley Junction along the towpath of the [Staffordshire and Worcestershire Canal](/wiki/Staffordshire_and_Worcestershire_Canal "Staffordshire and Worcestershire Canal") and then along the east bank towpath of the [Shropshire Union Canal](/wiki/Shropshire_Union_Canal "Shropshire Union Canal") as far as Pendeford Mill Lane before turning to [Bilbrook](/wiki/Bilbrook,_Staffordshire "Bilbrook, Staffordshire") in Staffordshire. The lanes of nearby South Staffordshire and east Shropshire provide ideal cycle touring conditions.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Music")]

The rock groups [Slade](/wiki/Slade "Slade"), [Cloven Hoof](/wiki/Cloven_Hoof_(band) "Cloven Hoof (band)"), [Sahotas](/wiki/Sahotas "Sahotas"), [The Mighty Lemon Drops](/wiki/The_Mighty_Lemon_Drops "The Mighty Lemon Drops") and [Babylon Zoo](/wiki/Babylon_Zoo "Babylon Zoo") came from Wolverhampton, as do [electronic musician](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") [Bibio](/wiki/Bibio "Bibio"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music")/[R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") singer [Beverley Knight](/wiki/Beverley_Knight "Beverley Knight"), [drum and bass](/wiki/Drum_and_bass "Drum and bass") guru [Goldie](/wiki/Goldie "Goldie"), and [roots reggae](/wiki/Roots_reggae "Roots reggae") maestro [Macka B](/wiki/Macka_B "Macka B"). [Kevin Rowland](/wiki/Kevin_Rowland "Kevin Rowland") of [Dexys Midnight Runners](/wiki/Dexys_Midnight_Runners "Dexys Midnight Runners") was born in Wednesfield, Wolverhampton.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Hip Hop](/wiki/Hip_Hop "Hip Hop") music producer [S-X](/wiki/S-X_(producer) "S-X (producer)") who has worked with [T.I.](/wiki/T.I. "T.I."), [J. Cole](/wiki/J._Cole "J. Cole"), [Birdman](/wiki/Birdman_(rapper) "Birdman (rapper)") & [Lil Wayne](/wiki/Lil_Wayne "Lil Wayne") was born and raised and still lives in Wolverhampton. In 2010, Wolverhampton-born singer [Liam Payne](/wiki/Liam_Payne "Liam Payne") (1993–2024) came third in the British television music show *[The X Factor](/wiki/The_X_Factor_(UK_series_7) "The X Factor (UK series 7)")* with his boy band [One Direction](/wiki/One_Direction "One Direction"), who in March 2012 became the first British group to go straight to the top of the [US music charts](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") with their debut album, *[Up All Night](/wiki/Up_All_Night_(One_Direction_album) "Up All Night (One Direction album)")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Wolverhampton has a number of live music venues; the largest occasionally used being the [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") ground, [Molineux Stadium](/wiki/Molineux_Stadium "Molineux Stadium"), which was used for a [Bon Jovi](/wiki/Bon_Jovi "Bon Jovi") concert in 2003,[[170]](#cite_note-170) but the biggest indoor venue regularly used is [Wolverhampton Civic Hall](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Civic_Hall "Wolverhampton Civic Hall"), with a standing capacity of 3,000.[[171]](#cite_note-171) Second to that is Wulfrun Hall (part of the same complex as the Civic Hall, which is owned and operated by the City Council) which has a standing capacity of just over 1,100.[[172]](#cite_note-172) The Civic Halls complex also has a newer venue, The Slade Rooms (named after the 1970s rock band), which has a capacity of approximately 550 standing. There are also a number of smaller venues with capacities of between 100 and 250, although the longest-established of these, the Wolverhampton Varsity, is now closed, as is the Little Civic. Other venues include the Light Bar in Fryer Street, the 'Numa Bar' and the Dog & Doublet (next to the old Little Civic), although the situation in this area of entertainment remains fluid. The 18th-century [St John's Church](/wiki/St_John%27s_Church,_Wolverhampton "St John's Church, Wolverhampton") is a venue for smaller scale classical concerts. The city is also home to Regent Records, a choral and organ music recording company, and Wolf Town DIY, an independent record label that primarily releases punk and alternative music by underground artists.[[173]](#cite_note-173) The Midland Box Office is the primary sales point for most of Wolverhampton's venues and is situated in Queen Square.

The city's main choral groups include the City of Wolverhampton Choir,[[174]](#cite_note-174) (a choral society founded as the Wolverhampton Civic Choir in 1947) and the Choir of [St. Peter's Collegiate Church](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Collegiate_Church,_Wolverhampton "St. Peter's Collegiate Church, Wolverhampton").

### Arts and museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Arts and museums")]

The [Grand Theatre](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Grand_Theatre "Wolverhampton Grand Theatre") on Lichfield Street is Wolverhampton's largest theatre, opening on 10 December 1894. It was designed by [C. J. Phipps](/wiki/Charles_J._Phipps "Charles J. Phipps") and completed within six months. Included amongst the people to have appeared at the theatre are [Henry Irving](/wiki/Henry_Irving "Henry Irving"), [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") and [Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery"). It was also used by politicians including [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") and [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"). The theatre was closed between 1980 and 1982.[[175]](#cite_note-175)

The [Arena Theatre](/wiki/Arena_Theatre,_Wolverhampton "Arena Theatre, Wolverhampton") on Wulfruna Street, within the [University of Wolverhampton](/wiki/University_of_Wolverhampton "University of Wolverhampton") is the secondary theatre, seating 150. It hosts both professional and amateur performances.[[176]](#cite_note-176)

Cinemas include a multiplex [Cineworld](/wiki/Cineworld "Cineworld") located at [Bentley Bridge](/wiki/Bentley_Bridge "Bentley Bridge"), [Wednesfield](/wiki/Wednesfield "Wednesfield"),[[177]](#cite_note-177) and the four-screen [Lockworks Cinema](/wiki/Light_House_Media_Centre "Light House Media Centre"), housed in the former [Chubb Buildings](/wiki/Chubb_Locks "Chubb Locks") on Fryer Street.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Wolverhampton_Art_Gallery_2.jpg/250px-Wolverhampton_Art_Gallery_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wolverhampton_Art_Gallery_2.jpg)

Wolverhampton Art Gallery

The city's Arts & Museums service, run by the council, covers three sites: [Wolverhampton Art Gallery](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Art_Gallery "Wolverhampton Art Gallery"), home to England's biggest [Pop art](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art") collection after that held at the [Tate](/wiki/Tate "Tate");[[179]](#cite_note-179) [Bantock House](/wiki/Bantock_House_Museum_and_Park "Bantock House Museum and Park"), a fine historic house with Edwardian interior with a museum of Wolverhampton located within Bantock Park;[[180]](#cite_note-180) [Bilston Craft Gallery](/wiki/Bilston_Craft_Gallery "Bilston Craft Gallery") with exhibitions of contemporary crafts.[[181]](#cite_note-181)

The [Black Country Living Museum](/wiki/Black_Country_Living_Museum "Black Country Living Museum"), situated in nearby [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley"), has a large collection of artefacts and buildings from across the [Black Country](/wiki/Black_Country "Black Country"), including an extensive collection associated with the city.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

[Eagle Works Studios and Gallery](/w/index.php?title=Eagle_Works_Studios_and_Gallery&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eagle Works Studios and Gallery (page does not exist)") situated in [Chapel Ash](/wiki/Chapel_Ash "Chapel Ash"), is a self run artists' group. It provides studio accommodation for eighteen visual artists, mostly painters. Its small gallery holds a regular programme of exhibitions to show and promote contemporary art in the city.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

The [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust_for_Places_of_Historic_Interest_or_Natural_Beauty "National Trust for Places of Historic Interest or Natural Beauty") owns two properties on the edge of the city that are open to the public: [Wightwick Manor](/wiki/Wightwick_Manor "Wightwick Manor"), which is a [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") [manor house](/wiki/Manor_house "Manor house") and one of only a few surviving examples of a house built and furnished under the influence of the [Arts and Crafts movement](/wiki/Arts_and_Crafts_movement "Arts and Crafts movement"),[[184]](#cite_note-greeves-184) and [Moseley Old Hall](/wiki/Moseley_Old_Hall "Moseley Old Hall"), which is famous as one of the resting places of [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") during his [escape to France](/wiki/Escape_of_Charles_II "Escape of Charles II") following defeat at the [Battle of Worcester](/wiki/Battle_of_Worcester "Battle of Worcester") in 1651.[[185]](#cite_note-185) [English Heritage](/wiki/English_Heritage "English Heritage") owns [Boscobel House](/wiki/Boscobel_House "Boscobel House"), within [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), another refuge of Charles II.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

Nearby museums also include the [Royal Air Force Museum](/wiki/Royal_Air_Force_Museum "Royal Air Force Museum"), at [RAF Cosford](/wiki/DCAE_Cosford "DCAE Cosford") and the RAF Fire Service Museum at [Wolverhampton Airport](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Airport "Wolverhampton Airport").,[[187]](#cite_note-187) whilst [Chillington Hall](/wiki/Chillington_Hall "Chillington Hall"), which boasts of grounds designed by [Capability Brown](/wiki/Capability_Brown "Capability Brown"),[[188]](#cite_note-188) and [Himley Hall](/wiki/Himley_Hall "Himley Hall") are nearby examples of houses open to the public.

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Libraries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Wolverhampton_Central_Library.jpg/250px-Wolverhampton_Central_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wolverhampton_Central_Library.jpg)

Wolverhampton Central Library

Located on the corner of Garrick Street and St George's Parade, Wolverhampton Central Library is a [Grade II listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"), designed by architect Henry T. Hare and opened in 1902. It was originally commissioned to commemorate [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom")'s Diamond Jubilee using funds raised by the Mayor, Alderman S Craddock, and by a grant of £1,000 from [Andrew Carnegie](/wiki/Andrew_Carnegie "Andrew Carnegie"). This new library improved public access to information and reading material, replacing its cramped predecessor in the old Garrick Street Police Station.[[189]](#cite_note-library-189)

The terracotta exterior has a tripartite theme of related, but distinct façades. The entrance façade is the architect's centrepiece and is decorated with a frieze under the triple window which carries the Royal Coat of Arms and the Wolverhampton Coat of Arms. The other two façades celebrate English literary giants; [Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer"), [Dryden](/wiki/John_Dryden "John Dryden"), [Pope](/wiki/Alexander_Pope "Alexander Pope"), [Shelley](/wiki/Percy_Bysshe_Shelley "Percy Bysshe Shelley"), [Byron](/wiki/George_Gordon_Byron,_6th_Baron_Byron "George Gordon Byron, 6th Baron Byron") and [Spenser](/wiki/Edmund_Spenser "Edmund Spenser") on one side and [Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton") and [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") on the other.[[189]](#cite_note-library-189) An extension for a newsroom and a students' room was added in 1936 followed by a small brick and concrete extension at the rear in the 1970s.[[189]](#cite_note-library-189)

Wolverhampton City Council also operate 14 branch libraries within the city.[[190]](#cite_note-190)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Media")]

Wolverhampton is home to the *[Express & Star](/wiki/Express_%26_Star "Express & Star")* newspaper, which boasts of having the largest circulation of any provincial daily evening newspaper in the UK.[[191]](#cite_note-191) Parent company [Midland News Association](/wiki/Midland_News_Association "Midland News Association") is based in Wolverhampton.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Local television news programmes are [BBC Midlands Today](/wiki/BBC_Midlands_Today "BBC Midlands Today") and [ITV News Central](/wiki/ITV_News_Central "ITV News Central").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") local radio station that covers the city is [BBC Radio WM](/wiki/BBC_Radio_WM "BBC Radio WM") on 95.6 FM.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The city was originally home to four radio stations, [107.7 The Wolf](/wiki/107.7_The_Wolf "107.7 The Wolf") which was absorbed into regional station [Signal 107](/wiki/Signal_107 "Signal 107"), now broadcasting as [Greatest Hits Radio Black Country & Shropshire](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_Black_Country_%26_Shropshire "Greatest Hits Radio Black Country & Shropshire") from studios in Liverpool, Manchester, London and Birmingham, Beacon Radio absorbed by [Orion Media](/wiki/Orion_Media "Orion Media") and now laterly [Bauer Media](/wiki/Bauer_Media "Bauer Media") broadcasting as [Hits Radio Black Country & Shropshire](/wiki/Hits_Radio_Black_Country_%26_Shropshire "Hits Radio Black Country & Shropshire") from studios in Birmingham and Manchester. The third was Radio [WABC](/wiki/Classic_Gold_WABC "Classic Gold WABC"), the AM service from Beacon Radio that has since closed following a number of rebrands as [Classic Gold Digital](/wiki/Classic_Gold_Digital "Classic Gold Digital"), [Gold](/wiki/Gold_(British_radio_network) "Gold (British radio network)") and [Free Radio](/wiki/Free_Radio_(network) "Free Radio (network)") 80s. The fourth, and now the only radio station based in the city, is community radio station [WCR FM](/wiki/WCR_FM "WCR FM") which broadcasts solely to the Wolverhampton on 101.8FM, online, via the Tune In app and via smart speaker.

In December 2005, the BBC commissioned the poet [Ian McMillan](/wiki/Ian_McMillan_(poet) "Ian McMillan (poet)") to write a poem about Wolverhampton, along with four other towns which apparently "had a reputation they didn't deserve".[[192]](#cite_note-192) Made in Wolverhampton (2013),[[193]](#cite_note-193) an essay [documentary](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") feature film by [Adam Kossoff](/wiki/Adam_Kossoff "Adam Kossoff"), maps out the hidden history and architecture of Wolverhampton from the point of view of a discontented fictional arts lecturer who teaches at the [Wolverhampton School of Art](/wiki/Wolverhampton_School_of_Art "Wolverhampton School of Art").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/13/University_of_Wolverhampton.jpg/250px-University_of_Wolverhampton.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Wolverhampton.jpg)

University of Wolverhampton

See also: [List of schools in Wolverhampton](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Wolverhampton "List of schools in Wolverhampton")

The [University of Wolverhampton](/wiki/University_of_Wolverhampton "University of Wolverhampton") is the main provider of higher education in the city. The university currently has more than 23,000 students. In 1835, the Wolverhampton Mechanics' Institute was founded, and its lineage can be traced via the Wolverhampton and Staffordshire Technical College (1935), to The Polytechnic, Wolverhampton (1969) to today's [University of Wolverhampton](/wiki/University_of_Wolverhampton "University of Wolverhampton"), given university status in 1992. The main university campus is in the city centre, with other campuses at [Compton](/wiki/Compton,_Wolverhampton "Compton, Wolverhampton"), and in the nearby towns of [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall") and [Telford](/wiki/Telford "Telford").

[Wolverhampton Grammar School](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Grammar_School "Wolverhampton Grammar School") was founded in 1512, making it one of the oldest active schools in the UK.[[194]](#cite_note-194) Old boys include [Mervyn King](/wiki/Mervyn_King_(economist) "Mervyn King (economist)"), Governor of the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") between July 2003 and 2013, and [Sir David Wright](/wiki/David_Wright_(diplomat) "David Wright (diplomat)"), former British Ambassador to Japan.

Other notably historic schools include [The Royal School, Wolverhampton](/wiki/The_Royal_School,_Wolverhampton "The Royal School, Wolverhampton") (founded in 1850),[[195]](#cite_note-195) and [Tettenhall College](/wiki/Tettenhall_College "Tettenhall College") (1863),[[196]](#cite_note-196) which educated the winner of [Nobel Prize for Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_for_Chemistry "Nobel Prize for Chemistry"), Professor Sir [Arthur Harden](/wiki/Arthur_Harden "Arthur Harden"). [City of Wolverhampton College](/wiki/City_of_Wolverhampton_College "City of Wolverhampton College") is the main [further education](/wiki/Further_education "Further education") college in the city.

[Wolverhampton Girls' High School](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Girls%27_High_School "Wolverhampton Girls' High School") is a well known selective school ([state](/wiki/State_school "State school") [grammar school](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school")) which has produced top of league table results within Wolverhampton.[[197]](#cite_note-197) Notable old girls include the former English women's cricket captain [Rachael Heyhoe-Flint](/wiki/Rachael_Heyhoe-Flint "Rachael Heyhoe-Flint") and [Baroness Hayman](/wiki/Helene_Hayman,_Baroness_Hayman "Helene Hayman, Baroness Hayman"), first [Lord Speaker](/wiki/Lord_Speaker "Lord Speaker") of the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), as well as Georgia Elwiss, a member of the current 2015 women's cricket team.

St Peter's Collegiate School was founded in 1847 in buildings adjacent to [St Peter's Collegiate Church](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_Collegiate_Church "St Peter's Collegiate Church") in Wolverhampton town centre. It moved to the present extensive green site at Compton Park in 1965. St Peter's is the oldest established educational institution currently in the state sector in Wolverhampton, with a tradition of academic, cultural and sporting excellence nourished by Christian spiritual and moral values. Previous students include record-breaking goalscoring footballer [Arthur Rowley](/wiki/Arthur_Rowley "Arthur Rowley").

Wolverhampton, unlike a number of nearby areas such as [Dudley](/wiki/Dudley "Dudley") and [South Staffordshire](/wiki/South_Staffordshire "South Staffordshire"), has always had [three-tier education](/wiki/Three-tier_education "Three-tier education"): 5–7 infants, 7–11 juniors and 11-16/18 secondary schools. Some secondary schools have sixth form facilities for children aged 16+.

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Molineux_Ground%2C_Wolverhampton.jpg/250px-Molineux_Ground%2C_Wolverhampton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Molineux_Ground,_Wolverhampton.jpg)

[Molineux Stadium](/wiki/Molineux_Stadium "Molineux Stadium"), home of Wolverhampton Wanderers

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Football")]

Wolverhampton is represented in the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), the highest tier of English football, by [Wolverhampton Wanderers FC](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_FC "Wolverhampton Wanderers FC"). 'Wolves', as they are known, are one of the oldest English football clubs, and were one of the 12 founder members of the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League"). Their most successful period was the 1950s, where they won three Football League Championships (then the highest division) and two [FA Cups](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup"), and were involved in the earliest European friendlies. They were hailed by the press as 'The Unofficial World Champions' after one of their most famous victories, against [Budapest Honvéd FC](/wiki/Budapest_Honv%C3%A9d_FC "Budapest Honvéd FC") of Hungary. They were also the first English team to play in the Soviet Union. These victories instigated the birth of the European Cup competition which later evolved into the UEFA Champions' League (see [European Cup and Champions League history](/wiki/European_Cup_and_Champions_League_history "European Cup and Champions League history")).

In total, they have won three Football League titles (prior to the top division becoming the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League")), four FA Cups, have two [League Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Cup "Football League Cup") victories and many other minor honours, including reaching the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") Final in 1972, and appearances in the last eight of both the [UEFA European Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), and the European [Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup"), but spent just one season in the top division between 1984 and 2009. They are also one of only two clubs, along with [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_FC "Portsmouth FC"), to have won five different league titles; they have championed all four tiers of the professional English league, as well as the long-defunct northern section of the Third Division.

Wolves have a long-established [rivalry](/wiki/Black_Country_derby "Black Country derby") with [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion "West Bromwich Albion"). Separated by 12 mi (19 km), the two clubs have faced each other over 160 times since 1886.[[198]](#cite_note-198) [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa "Aston Villa") and [Birmingham City FC](/wiki/Birmingham_City_FC "Birmingham City FC") are also close rivals of Wolves, having played them 121[[199]](#cite_note-199) and 136[[200]](#cite_note-200) times respectively. Geographically, [Walsall FC](/wiki/Walsall_FC "Walsall FC") are closest to Wolves, but rarely compete at the same level. Since 1886, the two clubs have only played 16 times against each other.[[201]](#cite_note-201)

Several other Wolverhampton-based clubs play [non-league](/wiki/Non-league "Non-league") football, notably [AFC Wulfrunians](/wiki/AFC_Wulfrunians "AFC Wulfrunians") and [Wolverhampton Sporting Community F.C.](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Sporting_C.F.C. "Wolverhampton Sporting C.F.C.") in the [Midland Football League](/wiki/Midland_Football_League_(2014) "Midland Football League (2014)") Premier Division, [Wolverhampton Casuals FC](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Casuals_FC "Wolverhampton Casuals FC"), [Wednesfield FC](/wiki/Wednesfield_FC "Wednesfield FC"), and [Bilston Town FC](/wiki/Bilston_Town_FC "Bilston Town FC") in the [West Midlands (Regional) League](/wiki/West_Midlands_(Regional)_League "West Midlands (Regional) League"), and [Warstones Wanderers F.C.](/wiki/Warstones_Wanderers_F.C. "Warstones Wanderers F.C.") in the West Midlands (Regional) League – Division Two.

### Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Wolverhampton's Aldersley Leisure Village is also home to [Wolverhampton & Bilston Athletics Club](/wiki/Wolverhampton_%26_Bilston_Athletics_Club "Wolverhampton & Bilston Athletics Club"), which was formed in 1967 with a merger between Wolverhampton Harriers and Bilston Town Athletic Club. They have won the National League Division One for men from 1975 to 1982, and the Men's National Cup finals in 1976, 1977, 1979 and 1980. It also represented Britain in the European Clubs Cup from 1976 to 1983 with the best finishing position of third.[[202]](#cite_note-202)

Olympic Medallists in athletics [Sonia Lannaman](/wiki/Sonia_Lannaman "Sonia Lannaman") and [Tessa Sanderson](/wiki/Tessa_Sanderson "Tessa Sanderson") lived within the city.[[203]](#cite_note-203)

### Cricket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Cricket")]

There are a number of [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") clubs in the city. These are Wolverhampton Cricket Club, Springhill Cricket Club, Springvale Cricket Club, Fordhouses Cricket Club and Wombourne Cricket Club.[[204]](#cite_note-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206)[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-208)

### Field hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Field hockey")]

The city has a few [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") hockey clubs that compete in the [Midlands Hockey League](/wiki/Midland_Regional_Hockey_Association "Midland Regional Hockey Association"). These are Wolverhampton & Tettenhall Hockey Club and Finchfield Hockey Club.[[209]](#cite_note-209)[[210]](#cite_note-210)[[211]](#cite_note-211)[[212]](#cite_note-212)

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Cycling")]

Wolverhampton Wheelers is the city's oldest cycling club, formed in 1891.[[213]](#cite_note-213) It was home to [Hugh Porter](/wiki/Hugh_Porter "Hugh Porter") who won four world championship pursuit titles; and [Percy Stallard](/wiki/Percy_Stallard "Percy Stallard") who has been credited with bringing cycle road racing to Britain when he held the Llangollen to Wolverhampton race on 7 June 1942.[[214]](#cite_note-214) Wolverhampton Wheelers make extensive use of the velodrome at Aldersley Stadium. Wolverhampton was also the home of [Trevor Gadd](/wiki/Trevor_Gadd "Trevor Gadd"), who was a six time British National Cycle champion and two-time silver medallist at the [1978 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1978_Commonwealth_Games "1978 Commonwealth Games"), as well as a fifth-place finisher in the [1977 UCI Track Cycling World Championships](/wiki/1977_UCI_Track_Cycling_World_Championships "1977 UCI Track Cycling World Championships") in Venezuela.

Wolverhampton has also hosted the [Tour of Britain](/wiki/Tour_of_Britain "Tour of Britain"), with a stage start in 2006, a stage finish in 2007 and a sprint finish in 2008.
It is also home to Wednesfield Aces cycle speedway who are based on [Ashmore Park](/wiki/Ashmore_Park "Ashmore Park").

### Horse and greyhound racing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Horse and greyhound racing")]

[Wolverhampton Racecourse](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Racecourse "Wolverhampton Racecourse") is located at Dunstall Park, just to the north of the city centre. This was one of the first all-weather [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") courses in the UK and is Britain's only floodlit horse race track. There is also [greyhound racing](/wiki/Greyhound_racing "Greyhound racing") at Monmore Green. [West Park](/wiki/West_Park,_Wolverhampton "West Park, Wolverhampton"), a large park near the city centre, was converted from a racecourse.

A horse by the name of [Wolverhampton](/wiki/1849_Grand_National#Leading_contenders "1849 Grand National") was among the leading contenders for the [1849 Grand National](/wiki/1849_Grand_National "1849 Grand National") at [Aintree](/wiki/Aintree "Aintree") but did not complete the course.

### Motor sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Motor sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Sunbeam_1000HP.jpg/250px-Sunbeam_1000HP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sunbeam_1000HP.jpg)

Sunbeam 1000HP at National Motor Museum in Beaulieu, UK

[Sunbeam](/wiki/Sunbeam_Motor_Car_Company "Sunbeam Motor Car Company") built many early [Grand Prix](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motor_racing "Grand Prix motor racing") cars and was the only British make to win a Grand Prix in the first half of the 20th century.[[215]](#cite_note-215) Sunbeam also built several holders of the [Land speed record](/wiki/Land_speed_record "Land speed record"), including the first vehicle to travel at over 200 miles per hour (322 km/h), the [Sunbeam 1000 hp](/wiki/Sunbeam_1000_hp "Sunbeam 1000 hp").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[AJS](/wiki/AJS "AJS") was heavily involved in motorcycle racing either side of World War II, which included winning the [1949 World Championship](/wiki/1949_Grand_Prix_motorcycle_racing_season "1949 Grand Prix motorcycle racing season") in the 500cc category.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Kieft Cars](/wiki/Kieft_Cars "Kieft Cars") built [Formula Three](/wiki/Formula_Three "Formula Three") cars in the early 1950s. Their best known driver was [Stirling Moss](/wiki/Stirling_Moss "Stirling Moss").[[216]](#cite_note-216)

[Wolverhampton Wolves](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wolves "Wolverhampton Wolves"), one of the leading [speedway](/wiki/Motorcycle_speedway "Motorcycle speedway") clubs in the UK represents the city, participating in the [Elite League](/wiki/Elite_League_(speedway) "Elite League (speedway)") at the Monmore Green stadium. Wolverhampton Speedway is one of the oldest speedway tracks in the world that is still in operation being first used, albeit briefly in 1928. The track re-opened in 1950 for a single meeting and in 1952 the Wasps competed in the Third Division on the National League. The track closed early in 1954 and did not re-open until 1961 when the Wolves were introduced to the Provincial League. The track has almost been an ever-present ever since and currently operates in the British Elite League.[[217]](#cite_note-217) [Ole Olsen](/wiki/Ole_Olsen_(speedway_rider) "Ole Olsen (speedway rider)") (in 1971 and 1975), [Sam Ermolenko](/wiki/Sam_Ermolenko "Sam Ermolenko") (in 1993) and [Tai Woffinden](/wiki/Tai_Woffinden "Tai Woffinden") (in 2013) were riders for the club when they became [World Speedway Champions](/wiki/Speedway_World_Championship "Speedway World Championship"). The Wolves are defending Elite League champions, having defeated the Belle Vue Aces in the 2016 play-off final.[[218]](#cite_note-218)

[Le Mans 24 Hours](/wiki/24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "24 Hours of Le Mans") winner [Richard Attwood](/wiki/Richard_Attwood "Richard Attwood") is from the city.

### Marathon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Marathon")]

Wolverhampton is home to the [Carver Wolverhampton City Marathon](/wiki/Carver_Wolverhampton_City_Marathon "Carver Wolverhampton City Marathon"). The marathon is part of a series of events whose main goal is to raise money for charity.

### Obstacle course race

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Obstacle course race")]

The [Tough Guy Race](/wiki/Tough_Guy_Race "Tough Guy Race") is held annually near Wolverhampton. The race is considered the first civilian obstacle course race.

### Commonwealth Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Commonwealth Games")]

The City hosted the Cycling Time Trials for the [2022 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2022_Commonwealth_Games "2022 Commonwealth Games") on 4 August 2022. The start and finish was at West Park, with the route passing through the City Centre before heading out into city suburbs.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wolverhampton&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Notable people")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Official_portrait_of_Baroness_Hayman_crop_2.jpg/250px-Official_portrait_of_Baroness_Hayman_crop_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_portrait_of_Baroness_Hayman_crop_2.jpg)

Baroness Hayman, 2018

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/BillyWrightStatue.jpg/250px-BillyWrightStatue.jpg)](/wiki/File:BillyWrightStatue.jpg)

Statue of [Billy Wright](/wiki/Billy_Wright_(footballer_born_1924) "Billy Wright (footballer born 1924)") outside [Molineux Stadium](/wiki/Molineux_Stadium "Molineux Stadium")

Main article: [List of people from Wolverhampton](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Wolverhampton "List of people from Wolverhampton")

Political figures associated with Wolverhampton include [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell") MP, Sir [Charles Pelham Villiers](/wiki/Charles_Pelham_Villiers "Charles Pelham Villiers") MP,[[220]](#cite_note-220) who holds the record for the longest serving MP, [Helene Hayman, Baroness Hayman](/wiki/Helene_Hayman,_Baroness_Hayman "Helene Hayman, Baroness Hayman"), who was the first [Lord Speaker](/wiki/Lord_Speaker "Lord Speaker") within the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), former Cabinet minister [Stephen Byers](/wiki/Stephen_Byers "Stephen Byers"), former [Prime Minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson"), who briefly worked as a writer for the *[Express & Star](/wiki/Express_%26_Star "Express & Star")*, [Henry Fowler, 1st Viscount Wolverhampton](/wiki/Henry_Fowler,_1st_Viscount_Wolverhampton "Henry Fowler, 1st Viscount Wolverhampton"), a solicitor and Liberal politician,[[221]](#cite_note-221) [David Wright](/wiki/David_Wright_(diplomat) "David Wright (diplomat)"), a former UK Ambassador to Japan, and [Button Gwinnett](/wiki/Button_Gwinnett "Button Gwinnett"), who was a signatory of the US [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and briefly served as [Governor of Georgia](/wiki/Governor_of_Georgia "Governor of Georgia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sportspeople associated with the city, include footballers [Billy Wright](/wiki/Billy_Wright_(footballer_born_1924) "Billy Wright (footballer born 1924)"), [Steve Bull](/wiki/Steve_Bull "Steve Bull"), [Bert Williams](/wiki/Bert_Williams_(footballer,_born_1920) "Bert Williams (footballer, born 1920)") and [Jimmy Mullen](/wiki/Jimmy_Mullen_(footballer_born_1923) "Jimmy Mullen (footballer born 1923)"); along with [Percy Stallard](/wiki/Percy_Stallard "Percy Stallard") and [Hugh Porter](/wiki/Hugh_Porter "Hugh Porter") from the world of cycling, the Olympic medallist swimmer [Anita Lonsbrough](/wiki/Anita_Lonsbrough "Anita Lonsbrough"), professional darts player [Wayne Jones](/wiki/Wayne_Jones_(darts_player) "Wayne Jones (darts player)"), racing driver and winner of the [24 hours of Le Mans](/wiki/24_hours_of_Le_Mans "24 hours of Le Mans"), [Richard Attwood](/wiki/Richard_Attwood "Richard Attwood") as well as athletes, [Tessa Sanderson](/wiki/Tessa_Sanderson "Tessa Sanderson") and [Denise Lewis](/wiki/Denise_Lewis "Denise Lewis"). Cricketer [Vikram Solanki](/wiki/Vikram_Solanki "Vikram Solanki") grew up here and played for Wolverhampton Cricket Club before joining [Worcestershire](/wiki/Worcestershire_County_Cricket_Club "Worcestershire County Cricket Club"). 2023 Wimbledon Boys' Singles Champion [Henry Searle](/wiki/Henry_Searle_(tennis) "Henry Searle (tennis)") is from Wolverhampton.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Entertainers include actors [Nigel Bennett](/wiki/Nigel_Bennett "Nigel Bennett"), [Goldie](/wiki/Goldie "Goldie"), [Frances Barber](/wiki/Frances_Barber "Frances Barber"), [Meera Syal](/wiki/Meera_Syal "Meera Syal") and [Eric Idle](/wiki/Eric_Idle "Eric Idle");[[222]](#cite_note-222) and musicians [Noddy Holder](/wiki/Noddy_Holder "Noddy Holder"), [Dave Hill](/wiki/Dave_Hill_(guitarist) "Dave Hill (guitarist)"), [Beverley Knight](/wiki/Beverley_Knight "Beverley Knight"), [Dave Holland](/wiki/Dave_Holland_(bassist) "Dave Holland (bassist)"), [Maggie Teyte](/wiki/Maggie_Teyte "Maggie Teyte"), [Edward Elgar](/wiki/Edward_Elgar "Edward Elgar"), [Robert Plant](/wiki/Robert_Plant "Robert Plant"), [Bibio](/wiki/Bibio "Bibio"), [Paul Raven](/wiki/Paul_Raven_(musician) "Paul Raven (musician)"), trap-metal rapper [Scarlxrd](/wiki/Scarlxrd "Scarlxrd"), and [Liam Payne](/wiki/Liam_Payne "Liam Payne") of the group [One Direction](/wiki/One_Direction "One Direction"); and television presenters [Suzi Perry](/wiki/Suzi_Perry "Suzi Perry"), [Mark Rhodes](/wiki/Mark_Rhodes "Mark Rhodes") and [Mark Speight](/wiki/Mark_Speight "Mark Speight"). The socially conservative future president of the [National Viewers' and Listeners' Association](/wiki/Mediawatch-UK "Mediawatch-UK"), [Mary Whitehouse](/wiki/Mary_Whitehouse "Mary Whitehouse"), lived in Wolverhampton between 1939 and the early 1960s before relocating to Shropshire.[[223]](#cite_note-shropstar-223)

Within the area of commerce and industry, Sir [Alfred Hickman](/wiki/Sir_Alfred_Hickman,_1st_Baronet "Sir Alfred Hickman, 1st Baronet") (first Chairman of [Tarmac](/wiki/Tarmac_Limited "Tarmac Limited")), Sir [Geoffrey Mander](/wiki/Geoffrey_Le_Mesurier_Mander "Geoffrey Le Mesurier Mander"), [John Marston](/wiki/John_Marston_(industrialist) "John Marston (industrialist)") founder of [Sunbeam Cycles](/wiki/Sunbeam_Cycles "Sunbeam Cycles") and [Sunbeam Motor Car Company](/wiki/Sunbeam_Motor_Car_Company "Sunbeam Motor Car Company"), [John 'Iron Mad' Wilkinson](/wiki/John_Wilkinson_(industrialist) "John Wilkinson (industrialist)") (pioneer of [Cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron")) and [Mervyn King](/wiki/Mervyn_King_(economist) "Mervyn King (economist)"), the former Governor of the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), are amongst the most notable. Prof [Ernest Geoffrey Cullwick](/wiki/Ernest_Geoffrey_Cullwick "Ernest Geoffrey Cullwick"), a specialist in electromagnetism and its effects on atomic particles, was born and raised in Wolverhampton. *Thief-Taker General*, [Jonathan Wild](/wiki/Jonathan_Wild "Jonathan Wild") (ca.1682–1725) came from the town.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

* [William Highfield Jones](/wiki/William_Highfield_Jones "William Highfield Jones"), 29 July 1902
* [Sir George Hayter Chubb](/wiki/George_Chubb,_1st_Baron_Hayter "George Chubb, 1st Baron Hayter"), 14 October 1909

* [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George") MP, 23 November 1918
